surfaces.evidence: wdt.rs:64 `WDT_CLOCK_HZ = crate::soc::chip::TCXO_HZ`; soc/ws63.rs:48 TCXO_HZ=24_000_000 (matches vendor watchdog_porting.h:15 CONFIG_WDT_CLOCK 24000000); soc/bs21.rs:29 TCXO_HZ=32_000_000 (no vendor WDT-clock citation available in-sandbox — fbb_bs2x absent; grep for CONFIG_WDT_CLOCK outside ws63 returns nothing).

audits.notes:
Driver: /home/sanchuanhehe/Documents/hispark-rs/hisi-riscv-rs/crates/hisi-riscv-hal/src/wdt.rs (chip-neutral; same code path for ws63-pac and bs2x-pac, whose WDT register layouts are byte-identical: 24-bit load[31:8], 3-bit rst_pl, 1-bit wdt_mode).

GENUINELY SAFE fields (no issue invented):
- ResetPulseLength enum -> rst_pl: 8 variants map exactly to the contiguous 3-bit field 0..=7 (ws63-pac lib.rs:23971 RstPlW<..,3>; regs_def rst_pl:3). Enum cannot exceed the field. OK.
- WdtMode enum -> wdt_mode bit7: 2 variants, both valid. DoubleInterrupt->1 matches vendor WDT_MODE_RESET_BY_SECOND_INTR=1 / PAC 'two interrupts then reset'. OK.
- reset_enable bool -> rst_en bit2, wdt_en bit0, wdt_imsk bit6: single bits, can't overflow. OK.
- WDT_RESTART (feed) / WDT_LOCK keys are full 32-bit registers; feed writes 0x1 (!=0x5A5A5A5A) which the regs_def says restarts the counter. OK.
- counter_value()/request_counter() poll WDT_CCVR_EN bit1 (wdt_ccvr_lock) after setting bit0 (wdt_ccvr_req); matches vendor get_left_time, though the Rust version has NO timeout on the poll loop (vendor caps at WAIT_CCVR_LOCK_MAX_CNT=2000) — a stuck lock bit would hang, but this is an operational-method robustness gap, not a config/construction surface, so out of audit scope.

PRECONDITIONS / ON-BOARD CONFIRMATION NEEDED:
1. clock gating: WDT is in the AON always-on domain; wdt_gate (AON_CRG_CKEN_CTL[4]) is assumed reset-default-on. Vendor turnon/turnoff_clk hooks are no-ops. Driver self-enables nothing (unlike PWM). Confirm AON gate is on out of reset on real silicon before relying on the 'construct->clocked' assumption.
2. BS2X WDT clock rate: 32 MHz TCXO assumption for BS21 is UNVERIFIED against vendor source (no fbb_bs2x in this tree). Confirm on a BS2X board that timeout_ms scales by 32 MHz, else the max-timeout clamp boundary (~134 s) and every conversion are wrong.

SEVERITY RANKING: the silent max-load clamp (class D, high) is the headline defect — it fails in the unsafe direction (watchdog fires sooner than asked) and is trivially reachable for any multi-minute timeout (>~179 s WS63 / >~134 s BS2X). The vendor signals these via ERRCODE_INVALID_PARAM; the reference-pattern fix is a construct-time-validated newtype (e.g. WdtTimeout::from_ms(u32)->Option, rejecting 0 and any value above the per-chip max instead of clamping), mirroring PwmPeriod.

surfaces.field: Config.baudrate (HIGH end — integer divider underflow to 0)
surfaces.issue: There is NO high-end guard. When baudrate > pclk/16 the integer divider div = pclk/(16*baud) computes to 0; the driver then writes div_l=0x00, div_h=0x00 and only a non-zero div_fra. A zero integer baud divisor is not a valid programming on the 16C550-style v151 core — the line baud clock is undefined/non-functional, so the UART silently produces no usable output (garbled or dead line). The driver never detects or signals this. The in-source comment 'valid baud in [153, 10_000_000]' is only true at the 160 MHz PLL base; at the DEFAULT-in-practice boot/BS2X base (clock_hz = 24/32/40 MHz, the documented common case for examples that skip clock_init) the true max is pclk/16 ≈ 1.5 Mbaud (24M), 2.0 Mbaud (32M/BS21), 2.5 Mbaud (40M). Any baud above that — all structurally valid u32 values that compile — give div=0. Verified numerically: at 40 MHz, baud=3_000_000 -> div=0; at 32 MHz (BS21), baud=3_000_000 -> div=0; at 160 MHz, baud=20_000_000 -> div=0.
surfaces.evidence: Vendor hal_uart_v151.c:392 `uint16_t brd_i = uart_clk / (baud << 4)` (= the integer divider written to DIV_L/DIV_H); div_l/div_h are each 8-bit (ws63-pac lib.rs:6789/6746, bs2x-pac lib.rs:2774/2733 FieldWriter<,8>) giving a 16-bit integer divider whose minimum meaningful value is 1, not 0. Driver uart.rs:128-132 reproduces brd_i as div=div64>>6 but only clamps the LOW end (line 122-123).

surfaces.field: Config.clock_hz (Option mismatch vs actual base -> wrong baud, AND it shifts the unguarded high-end limit)
surfaces.issue: clock_hz=None selects the 160 MHz PLL base, but on real silicon any example that does not run the XIP-unsafe clock_init still runs on flashboot's raw TCXO base (24/32/40 MHz). Passing None there yields a baud off by the PLL/TCXO ratio (~4x on a 40 MHz board, confirmed on silicon, issues #10/#15) — a structurally valid Config that silently produces a wrong, unreadable baud. The type is a bare Option<u32> with no link to whether clock_init has actually run, so the caller can trivially mismatch it. This also interacts with the high-end div=0 defect: the usable baud ceiling is pclk/16, so the SAME Config (e.g. 921600 or higher) can be fine at 160 MHz but underflow to div=0 at the 24/40 MHz boot base.
surfaces.evidence: uart.rs:41-49 doc + line 121 `config.clock_hz.unwrap_or(UART_CLOCK_HZ)`; soc/ws63.rs:70 UART_CLOCK_HZ=160e6 vs uart_boot_clock_hz()=24/40e6 (ws63.rs:90-98, reads HW_CTL 0x4000_0014); soc/bs21.rs:35 UART_CLOCK_HZ=32e6 marked TODO/assumed. Vendor uart_porting.h:30 UART_CLOCK_FRQ=24000000 default, switched by uart_port_set_clock_value (uart_porting.c:121).

surfaces.issue: configure_uart programs only the divider and frame registers; it never enables the UART clock gate and never sets pinmux, unlike the PWM reference driver which self-enables its full clock tree in configure ('construct -> clocked'). new_uart0 happens to work only because flashboot leaves UART0 (the boot console) already clocked and pinmuxed. Constructing UART1 or UART2 with a perfectly valid Config compiles and runs but produces no output on silicon because their CKEN_CTL1 gate is off and their TXD/RXD pads are not muxed to the UART function. Silent dead peripheral.
surfaces.evidence: uart.rs has zero references to clock/cken/iomux/pinmux (grep confirms). HAL knows the gates: clock.rs:64-66 Uart0/1/2 -> CKEN_CTL1 bits 18/19/20. Vendor requires both clock + pinmux: uart_porting.c:152 uart_port_config_pinmux writes UART{0,1,2}_TXD_SEL/RXD_SEL; hal_uart_v151.c:121 set_baud_rate is preceded by the porting clock/pinmux init. PWM (the reference) self-enables CKEN_CTL0[10:2]+DIV_CTL3 per pwm_port_clock_enable; UART does not.

surfaces.field: Config.baudrate (LOW end — silent low-clamp instead of signalling)
surfaces.issue: baudrate below min_baud (= pclk/(16*65535)+1, e.g. 153 at 160 MHz, 39 at 40 MHz, 31 at 32 MHz) is silently raised to min_baud rather than rejected. A caller asking for 50 baud at 160 MHz gets ~153 baud with no error — a structurally valid request that runs at the wrong rate. The clamp itself is correct as overflow protection (keeps div <= 0xFFFF), but it should be a construction-time None (PWM/I2S-style newtype) instead of a silent wrap.
surfaces.evidence: uart.rs:122-123 `let min_baud = (pclk/(16*65535))+1; let baudrate = if config.baudrate < min_baud { min_baud } else { config.baudrate };` — masks the request, returns nothing. 16-bit divider cap = div_h:div_l (8+8 bits, ws63-pac lib.rs:6746/6789).

surfaces.issue: StopBits::Two sets uart_ctl.stp (bit 7). Per the v151 hardware, stp=1 means 2 stop bits ONLY when the data length is not 5; with 5 data bits (dlen=0) stp=1 actually selects 1.5 stop bits. So the structurally valid combo DataBits::Five + StopBits::Two silently configures a 5-data/1.5-stop frame instead of the requested 5-data/2-stop. No error, just a frame mismatch with the peer.
surfaces.evidence: Vendor hal_uart_v151_regs_def.h:77-80 `stp : 1 ... 0x1 : 1.5 stop bits when DLEN is zero, else 2 stop bit`. Mirrored in ws63-pac lib.rs:6679 doc 'Stop bits: 0=1bit; 1=1.5/2bit'. Driver uart.rs:155-157 sets bit7 unconditionally for StopBits::Two regardless of data_bits.

audits.notes:
Field-width overflow (class A) is NOT a real risk here: configure_uart writes div_l/div_h/div_fra via raw w.bits() but pre-masks with &0xFF/&0xFF/&0x3F (uart.rs:130-132), and the low-end clamp guarantees the integer divider <= 0xFFFF, so neither the 8-bit DIV_L/DIV_H nor the 6-bit DIV_FRA can be truncated. dlen[3:2] (2-bit) only ever receives 0..3, parity/stop are single bits — all safe. div_fra rounding matches the vendor exactly (verified numerically across 160/40/32/24 MHz; the driver's `div64&0x3F` equals vendor brd_f in every sampled row), so div_fra is NOT a defect. The DataBits/Parity/StopBits enums can only hold valid encodings — no issue there.

The dominant, highest-value defect is the HIGH-end baud underflow (div=0): it is completely unguarded, hits realistic high-baud requests (>~1.5-2.5 Mbaud) at the boot/BS2X clock that callers WILL use, and is silent. Combined with the clock_hz Option mismatch it is the same family as the PWM 'type encodes measured hardware reality' lesson: a BaudRate newtype with a fallible constructor that takes the actual pclk and returns None unless 1 <= pclk/(16*baud) <= 65535 would make every constructible value programmable.

ON-BOARD CONFIRMATION NEEDED: (1) the exact usable max baud — whether silicon tolerates div=1 with a large div_fra or needs div>=2; (2) BS21/BS2X UART_CLOCK_HZ is marked TODO/assumed=32 MHz (soc/bs21.rs:34-35) — the high-end ceiling and all BS2X dividers depend on confirming it; (3) whether UART1/UART2 clock gate + pinmux are truly off at app entry (flashboot only provisions the UART0 console), which determines the severity of the missing self-enable.

SCOPE NOTE (not a config-value defect, flagging for completeness): the operational methods write_byte/read_byte/write take a runtime `idx: u8` independent of the type-state instance, so e.g. a Uart<Uart1> can be told to write idx=0 — a logic footgun, but not a 'compiles-but-unrunnable Config value' so excluded from the surfaces above.

surfaces.issue: The done-wait is an UNBOUNDED `while r.rpt_gadc_data_3().read().bits() & 0x1 == 0 { spin_loop() }` with no timeout/escape. On silicon, any condition that prevents the GADC from asserting done -- LDO/VREF not actually settled (only fixed delay_us, never an ack), AFE s2d handshake not completed, wrong clk_div, an un-provisioned analog input, or an efuse-trim-dependent block that never starts -- hangs the core forever (and silently, since QEMU returns a fixed sample so the hang is invisible in emulation). This is the single most dangerous surface: a structurally valid, fully-compiling call (e.g. read(Ain0)) can deadlock the whole application. The discarded-first-sample path runs convert_once twice, doubling the exposure on the very first call after power-up when settling is least certain.
surfaces.evidence: Authoritative vendor pattern bounds EVERY status-wait loop: fbb_ws63 .../hal/adc/v154/hal_adc_v154.c:33 `#define COUNT_THRESHOLD 1000` and lines 80-84, 108-112, 125-129, 143-147, 164-168, 171-176, 328-337 all use `while (...sts() != 1) { if (cnt > COUNT_THRESHOLD) break; cnt++; }`. Driver: gadc.rs:199-201 has no such bound. The PAC rpt_gadc_data_3 is an undecoded u32 (bs2x-pac/src/lib.rs:19341 `mod rpt_gadc_data_3`, `type Ux = u32`), so nothing else guards the read.

surfaces.field: PMU_AFE_DIG_PWR_EN pwr_ack (RO bit2) and PMU_AFE_GADC_CFG done (RO bit4)
surfaces.issue: The power-up sequence advances purely on fixed delay_us() busy-waits and never polls the acknowledge bits it documents itself (PMU_AFE_DIG_PWR_EN[2]=pwr_ack RO at line 37, PMU_AFE_GADC_CFG[4]=done RO at line 36). delay_us is a hand-rolled `us*64` spin sized for a 64 MHz core (line 78-85); if the app core runs at a different frequency, or the MTCMOS rail / s2d handshake takes longer than the hard-coded 30/50/150 us, new() returns a Gadc that LOOKS powered but whose analog front-end is not actually live. Every subsequent read() then either returns garbage or wedges on the unbounded done-poll above. The whole power-up is an unenforced precondition with no failure signal -- new() is infallible (-> Self) and cannot report a failed bring-up.
surfaces.evidence: Driver self-documents the RO ack bits at gadc.rs:36-37 but never reads them; bring-up uses only delay_us at lines 122,123,126(implicit),136,139,142,155,157. Vendor v154 gates analog bring-up on real status reads (hal_adc_v154.c:80,108,125,143,164 wait on *_cali_sts/gain_unit before proceeding) rather than on bare delays, and even those reads are bounded. fbb_bs2x v153 source is not present in this checkout to cite the exact ack registers, so the precise poll target needs on-board confirmation.

surfaces.field: absolute base addresses GADC_BASE 0x5703_6000, PMU_AFE_BASE 0x5700_8700, AON_AFE_ISO 0x5702_C230
surfaces.issue: The driver reaches the ANA/LDO and PMU-AFE sub-blocks via hard-coded raw absolute addresses (BS2X memory map) instead of the PAC, and there is NO chip gating -- the module is documented `chip-bs21` only but nothing in the type system or a cfg-gate prevents it from being constructed/compiled for WS63. On WS63 these addresses are not the GADC AFE (WS63 uses the LSADC v154 block at a different base, ADC_BASE 0x4400_C000 per the porting layer), so new() would scribble on unrelated/unmapped MMIO and read() would deadlock or fault. The bs2x-pac Gadc peripheral itself is at 0x5703_6000 (matches), but the two off-PAC sub-blocks are unguarded magic constants.
surfaces.evidence: gadc.rs:30-32 raw consts; module doc lines 1-3 say BS2X-only `chip-bs21`. WS63 ADC lives elsewhere: fbb_ws63 adc_porting.c:21 `#define ADC_BASE_ADDR 0x4400C000`. No `#[cfg(...)]` gate is present on the module or new(); construction is gated only by owning the GadcPeriph token, which exists in bs2x-pac.

surfaces.issue: rpt_gadc_data_2 is read as a raw u32, masked to 18 bits (raw & 0x3FFFF) and sign-extended at bit 17, but the PAC models the register as an undecoded 32-bit blob with no field info, and the module headline calls this a '13-bit GADC'. The 18-bit signed interpretation is the ACCUMULATED/averaged-output width (the driver selects diag node cfg_tst_1=2 = ACCUMULATED_AVERAGE_OUTPUT at line 149), not the raw 13-bit conversion. If on silicon the accumulator/average width differs (e.g. bits[17:13] carry status/overflow flags, or the true sign bit is not bit 17, or with a different accumulation count `satrt_cnt` than the v154's 0x18 the magnitude scales differently), every returned sample is silently mis-scaled or wrong-signed with no signal -- this is exactly the PWM-high-half class of 'the type encodes the datasheet, not measured silicon'. The mask/sign-extend math itself is correct and well-tested in host tests; the risk is the assumed 18-bit-signed-at-bit-17 layout.
surfaces.evidence: gadc.rs:217-219 sign_extend18 masks 0x3FFFF, sign bit 1<<17; module doc line 1 '13-bit GADC'. PAC: bs2x-pac/src/lib.rs:19312 `mod rpt_gadc_data_2` is fieldless (`type Ux=u32`, only a Debug printing bits()), so the PAC gives no confirmation of the 18-bit/sign-bit-17 layout. v154 uses an explicit accumulation count (hal_adc_v154_regs_op.c:74 `satrt_cnt = 0x18`, equ_model_sel = AVERAGE_OF_EIGHT_SAMPLES) governing output magnitude; the bs2x v153 accumulation width must be confirmed on board.

surfaces.issue: The common clock divider is written as a fixed magic 0x27 and marked `TODO(bs2x): COMMON_DEFAULT clk div`. 0x27 lands entirely in the ana_div_th[11:0] field (value 39) with prechg_div_th[23:12]=0. A zero prechg divider, or a wrong ana divider for the actual GADC source clock, is a structurally-valid combo that can produce no usable conversion clock (analog never finishes -> feeds the unbounded done-poll hang). The value is unvalidated and acknowledged as a guess in-source. There is no constructor input for it, so a caller cannot even correct it; it is hard-wired but unverified against silicon.
surfaces.evidence: gadc.rs:145 `r.cfg_clk_div_0().write(|w| w.bits(0x27)); // TODO(bs2x): COMMON_DEFAULT clk div`. PAC field widths: bs2x-pac/src/lib.rs cfg_clk_div_0 ana_div_th = Bits 0:11, prechg_div_th = Bits 12:23 (12-bit each), so 0x27 sets ana_div=0x27, prechg_div=0 -- the prechg divider being 0 is suspicious and unconfirmed. fbb_bs2x COMMON_DEFAULT value is not available in this checkout to confirm.

surfaces.issue: delay_us assumes a 64 MHz app core (`cycles = us.saturating_mul(64)`) and a roughly 1-cycle spin_loop. Every analog settling delay in the power-up (30/50/150 us) and in convert_once (5 us) is sized by this. If the BS2X boot/app clock is not 64 MHz (e.g. running on the 32 MHz XO during early boot, or a different PLL setting), all settling times are wrong by that ratio -- too-short delays mean the LDO/VREF/AFE are sampled before they are stable, producing wrong samples or, combined with the unbounded poll, a hang. The frequency is an unenforced precondition baked into a private helper with no way for the caller to declare the actual clock.
surfaces.evidence: gadc.rs:78-85 `let cycles = us.saturating_mul(64)`. The vendor uses a calibrated timer (uapi_tcxo_delay_us) for the equivalent settling waits (hal_adc_v154.c:64-69 DELAY_500US/DELAY_300US via tcxo), not a CPU-cycle spin, precisely so the delay is clock-independent. Lesson mirrors prior silicon finding that BS2X boot clock can be the TCXO, not the nominal core clock.

audits.notes:
Scope/ground-truth caveat: this driver claims to mirror the fbb_bs2x `hal_adc_v153` driver, but the fbb_bs2x SDK is NOT present in this checkout -- only fbb_ws63 (which ships the *v154 LSADC*, a different IP block at base 0x4400_C000). I cross-referenced against (a) the bs2x-pac register/field widths and (b) the v154 vendor driver as the nearest authoritative HiSilicon ADC pattern. Findings that depend on exact v153 values (the cfg_clk_div_0 default, the PMU ack-bit registers, and the 18-bit accumulator/sign layout) are flagged as needing on-board confirmation.

What is genuinely SAFE (no issue invented): (1) AdcChannel is a closed repr(u8) enum Ain0..Ain7 -> `1 << (channel as u32)` only ever sets bits 0..7, well inside the 11-bit amuxp_sensor_ch_sel[22:12] field; this correctly encodes the vendor's `channel >= ADC_CHANNEL_MAX_NUM` guard at the type level (adc.c:121). (2) The cfg_amux_1 packing is masked to 0x7FF per sub-field and matches the PAC layout amuxn[10:0]/bit11/amuxp[22:12]/bit23 exactly (verified against bs2x-pac cfg_amux_1 accessors). (3) sign_extend18 arithmetic is correct and exhaustively host-tested. (4) No classic class-A field truncation occurs from the driver's own writes: every register it writes is `type Ux = u32` written via raw `.bits()`, and cfg_clken=0x0011_1111 / cfg_rstn=0x0001_1111 align with the PAC's 1-bit-per-nibble field map (bits 0/4/8/12/16/20). The defects are all clock/precondition/measured-width (B/C) class, not PAC-overflow.

Severity ranking for remediation: the UNBOUNDED done-poll in convert_once is the top priority -- it is the only finding that can deadlock the core from a fully-valid call, and the vendor pattern (bounded COUNT_THRESHOLD loops) shows the intended fix. The infallible new() with delay-only (no ack-poll) power-up is the second; consider making new() return Result and polling the documented pwr_ack/done RO bits with a bounded loop.

surfaces.issue: A Master with all three dividers left at their Default (0) compiles and is structurally valid, but produces NO bit clock / frame sync on silicon. The vendor never lets the user pass raw dividers: hal_sio_v151_i2s_set_fs computes fs_div_num = data_width*channels and fs_div_ratio = (data_width*channels)/2, and bclk_div_num comes from sio_porting_get_bclk_div_num() (a real computed value, e.g. 12288000/(48000*16*2)). A zero divider means the FS/BCLK frequency-dividers output nothing usable. Default config (role=Master, all dividers=0) is the canonical broken case. Per the reference pattern, a Master constructor must REQUIRE non-zero dividers (type-state); a zero-divider Master should be unrepresentable.
surfaces.evidence: fbb_ws63 .../hal_sio_v151/hal_sio_v151.c:124-150 (hal_sio_v151_i2s_set_fs / hal_i2s_set_bclk compute the dividers, never accept 0); sio_porting.c:145-158 sio_porting_get_bclk_div_num; I2sConfig::default() in i2s.rs:116-118 ships bclk_div=0/fs_div_num=0/fs_div_ratio=0 with role=Master.

surfaces.field: i2s_crg "clk_en" write (configure writes 0x100 = bit8 in master mode)
surfaces.issue: configure() writes i2s_crg = 0x100 intending bit8 'clk_en'. The PAC SVD models i2s_crg as bclk_div[0:7]/clk_en@bit8/clk_sel@bit9 -- this is a FABRICATED textbook layout that does NOT match silicon. The vendor sio_v151_i2s_crg_data_t is: i2s_bclk_div_en@bit0, i2s_crg_clken@bit1, i2s_bclk_sel@bit2, i2s_fs_sel@bit4. So 0x100 sets a RESERVED bit and the real clock-enables (bit0 bclk_div_en, bit1 crg_clken) are NEVER set. Even a fully-configured Master emits no clock: the BCLK divider is never enabled. Vendor sequence: set bclk_div_en=0, write divider, set bclk_div_en=1, then crg_clken=1. PAC SVD must be fixed (vendor regs_def is authoritative), then driver must drive bits 0/1.
surfaces.evidence: fbb_ws63 hal_sio_v151_regs_def.h:385-395 (i2s_crg: bclk_div_en bit0, crg_clken bit1, bclk_sel bit2, fs_sel bit4); regs_op.h:1144-1225 confirms; hal_sio_v151.c:131-139 hal_i2s_set_bclk drives bclk_div_en. Contradicts ws63-pac/src/lib.rs:20967-21011 (i2s_crg clk_en@bit8). Driver i2s.rs:189-196.

surfaces.issue: configure() packs the mode register as channels[0:1], clk_edge@bit2, master@bit3, pcm@bit4, following the PAC SVD. The PAC SVD mode layout is FABRICATED and wrong vs silicon. Vendor sio_v151_mode_data_t is: mode(i2s/pcm)@bit0, pcm_mode@bit1, rxws_select@bit2, ext_rec_en@bit3, chn_num@bits[4:5], clk_edge@bit6, cfg_i2s_ms_mode_sel(master/slave)@bit7. Consequences on silicon: writing 'Master' (Rust bit3) actually sets ext_rec_en (multi-channel receive); the real master/slave select (bit7) is NEVER written, so the part stays a slave; channel count written into bits[0:1] corrupts the i2s/pcm-mode selects; clk_edge lands on rxws_select. Almost every structurally-valid mode config is silently misprogrammed. PAC SVD must be regenerated against the vendor regs_def.
surfaces.evidence: fbb_ws63 hal_sio_v151_regs_def.h:47-71 (mode bit0..bit7 assignment); regs_op.h:82-225 (set_mode/pcm_mode/ext_rec_en/chn_num/clk_edge/cfg_i2s_ms_mode_sel via the union order); hal_sio_v151.c:104-110,141-159. Contradicts ws63-pac/src/lib.rs:20181-20239 (channels@0:1, clk_edge@2, master_slave@3, pcm_mode@4). Driver i2s.rs:147-160.

surfaces.issue: The enum maps Six=2 and Eight=3, but the chn_num field on silicon codes 00=2ch, 01=4ch, 10=8ch, 11=16ch -- there is NO 6-channel mode. ChannelCount::Six (code 2) actually selects 8 channels and ChannelCount::Eight (code 3) selects 16 channels. A caller asking for 6 channels silently gets 8; asking for 8 silently gets 16. Compounded by the mode-register bit-position bug above, the value also lands in the wrong bits, but even the value->meaning mapping is wrong on its own.
surfaces.evidence: fbb_ws63 hal_sio_v151_regs_def.h:60-64 (chn_num: 00=2ch,01=4ch,10=8ch,11=16ch); hal_sio.h:169-178 (hal_sio_channel_number_t TWO_CH=0,FOUR_CH=1,EIGHT_CH=2,SIXTEEN_CH=3). Rust enum i2s.rs:46-55. PAC doc string lib.rs:20181 ('2=6ch;3=8ch') is also wrong.

surfaces.issue: The DataWidth enum exposes 8 codes (Bits8=0,Bits10,Bits12,Bits14,Bits16=4,Bits18,Bits20,Bits24=7) but the SIO IP data_width_set field is only 3 bits and the silicon enum is RESERVED=0, SIXTEEN_BIT=1, EIGHTEEN_BIT=2, TWENTY_BIT=3, TWENTY_FOUR_BIT=4, THIRTY_TWO_BIT=5. So: code 0 (Rust Bits8) is RESERVED on hardware (no valid width); Rust Bits16=4 actually programs 24-bit; Rust Bits24=7 is out of the defined 0..5 set entirely (undefined). The 8/10/12/14-bit widths the API advertises do not exist in this IP. Every DataWidth value is misinterpreted by silicon; the default tx/rx=Bits16 actually selects 24-bit samples.
surfaces.evidence: fbb_ws63 hal_sio.h:147-160 (hal_sio_data_width_t RESERVED=0..THIRTY_TWO_BIT=5); hal_sio_v151_regs_def.h:275-282 (data_width_set tx_mode/rx_mode 3 bits each). PAC tx_mode/rx_mode width=3 lib.rs:20791. Rust enum i2s.rs:59-76 with fabricated 8/10/12/14-bit codes.

surfaces.issue: i2s_bclk_div_num is a 7-bit field (max 127). bclk_div is a u8 and configure() masks it with &0x7F, so any value 128..=255 is SILENTLY TRUNCATED (wraps modulo 128) rather than rejected -- e.g. bclk_div=200 programs 72, a different and likely wrong clock. Should be a construct-time-validated newtype (0..=127) returning None on overflow, per the PWM PwmPeriod pattern.
surfaces.evidence: fbb_ws63 hal_sio_v151_regs_def.h:402-408 (bclk_div_num : 7); PAC BclkDivNumW FieldWriter width 7 lib.rs:21039. Driver mask i2s.rs:183.

surfaces.issue: i2s_fs_div_num is a 10-bit field (max 1023). fs_div_num is a u16 and configure() masks it &0x3FF, so values 1024..=65535 are SILENTLY TRUNCATED instead of signalled. Should be a validated newtype (0..=1023).
surfaces.evidence: fbb_ws63 hal_sio_v151_regs_def.h:415-421 (fs_div_num : 10); PAC FsDivNumW width 10 lib.rs:21080. Driver mask i2s.rs:184.

surfaces.issue: i2s_fs_div_ratio_num is an 11-bit field (max 2047). fs_div_ratio is a u16 masked &0x7FF, so values 2048..=65535 are SILENTLY TRUNCATED. Should be a validated newtype (0..=2047).
surfaces.evidence: fbb_ws63 hal_sio_v151_regs_def.h:428-434 (fs_div_ratio_num : 11); PAC FsDivRatioNumW width 11 lib.rs:21121. Driver mask i2s.rs:185.

surfaces.field: I2S clock gate (CKEN_CTL0 bit11 bus + bit12 clk) and CMU_NEW_CFG0 bit0
surfaces.issue: Neither new() nor configure() enables the I2S/SIO peripheral clock gate. The vendor sio_porting_clock_enable() sets CMU_NEW_CFG0 bit0 (CMU_DIV_AD_RSTN_SYNC), then CKEN @0x44001100 bit12 (I2S_CLKEN) and bit11 (I2S_BUS_CLKEN). The HAL even has Peripheral::I2s => CKEN_CTL0 (idx0) bit12, with bit11 documented as the bus gate, but i2s.rs never references clock/Peripheral::I2s/cken_info. So unless the caller separately gates the clock, the entire SIO MMIO block is unclocked and writes/reads do nothing. PWM self-enables its tree in configure(); I2S must do the same (bus gate + clk gate + CMU sync).
surfaces.evidence: fbb_ws63 sio_porting.c:85-96 (clock_enable sets CMU_NEW_CFG0 bit0, 0x44001100 bit12 I2S_CLKEN, bit11 I2S_BUS_CLKEN); HAL clock.rs:63 Peripheral::I2s=>Some((0,12)) with bit11 bus noted at clock.rs:57-63. i2s.rs has no clock reference (grep).

surfaces.issue: The FIFO threshold fields are 8 bits (PAC accepts 0..=255) but the physical SIO FIFO is only 8 entries deep (SIO_FIFO_SIZE=8). A threshold > 8 can never be reached, so the threshold interrupt silently never fires -- accepted by the type, unrunnable in practice. The driver's own Default sets threshold=8 (== full depth); the vendor uses rx=7/tx=4. Thresholds should be bounded to the FIFO depth (validated newtype) rather than accepting the full 8-bit field.
surfaces.evidence: fbb_ws63 hal_sio_v151.c:19-21 (SIO_FIFO_SIZE 8, SIO_RX_THRESHOLD 7, SIO_TX_THRESHOLD 4); fifo_threshold fields 8-bit each hal_sio_v151_regs_def.h:233-240 / PAC lib.rs:20656-20679. Driver mask/default i2s.rs:119-120,176.

surfaces.issue: When loopback=true, configure() does an unconditional write of (1<<8) to the version register. version is a mostly read-only register (bits0:7 = IP version, bit8 = loop); a blind write (vs read-modify-write) is fragile and the vendor instead does a RMW (hal_sio_v151_version_set_loop) and only under CONFIG_I2S_SUPPORT_LOOPBACK, paired with a dedicated txrx-enable sequence (hal_sio_v151_txrx_enable, which also toggles crg_clock_enable). A loopback config set here without that enable sequence + clock gate produces no working loop on silicon. Lower severity because the loop bit position matches, but it is not self-sufficient.
surfaces.evidence: fbb_ws63 hal_sio_v151_regs_def.h:31-40 (version[0:7] + loop@bit8); hal_sio_v151.c:342-403 (loop is RMW + requires txrx_enable/crg clock). PAC version loopback_mode@bit8 lib.rs:20142-20160. Driver blind write i2s.rs:163-167.

audits.notes:
ROOT CAUSE / systemic: The ws63-pac SVD for the i2s peripheral encodes a fabricated 'textbook' register layout that contradicts the authoritative vendor regs_def (hal_sio_v151_regs_def.h). The WS63 I2S is implemented over the SIO v151 IP (drivers/drivers/hal/sio/hal_sio_v151 + drivers/chips/ws63/porting/sio). Per the repo's QEMU-not-reference policy and the prior SPI_WSR lesson, the vendor regs_def is truth and the PAC SVD must be regenerated before the driver can be trusted. Two registers are wrong in the PAC and propagate into the driver: (1) mode register -- silicon is mode@b0, pcm_mode@b1, rxws_select@b2, ext_rec_en@b3, chn_num@b4:5, clk_edge@b6, master/slave@b7; PAC/driver use channels@b0:1, clk_edge@b2, master@b3, pcm@b4. (2) i2s_crg -- silicon is bclk_div_en@b0, crg_clken@b1, bclk_sel@b2, fs_sel@b4; PAC/driver use clk_en@b8 (reserved on silicon). Net effect: in the current driver a 'Master' device stays a slave (master bit7 never written) and emits no clock (crg enables at b0/b1 never set), so essentially no structurally-valid Master config is runnable.

Needs on-board confirmation (cannot be settled from headers alone): (a) DataWidth -- silicon enum tops out at 32-bit=code5 with code0 RESERVED; confirm whether 32-bit (code5) actually latches and which codes are usable on this part (parallel to the PWM pwm_freq-high-half lesson). (b) Whether bit11 (bus) AND bit12 (clk) of CKEN_CTL0 are BOTH required, plus CMU_NEW_CFG0 bit0, for the SIO block to respond -- sio_porting.c sets all three. (c) Effective FIFO depth for threshold bounding (vendor SIO_FIFO_SIZE=8). (d) Whether i2s_fs_sel/i2s_bclk_sel (crg b2/b4, both written 0 by vendor) must be explicitly cleared.

Driver also has no enable for the I2S IRQ line (vendor I2S_IRQN / irq_sio0_handler) but that is operational, not a config surface. The operational read/write (write_left/right u32, read_left/right) match the 32-bit left/right data regs and are fine. Recommended fix shape mirrors PWM: validated newtypes for bclk_div(0..=127)/fs_div_num(0..=1023)/fs_div_ratio(0..=2047)/fifo thresholds(0..=8); a Master constructor that requires non-zero dividers (type-state) so a zero-divider Master is unrepresentable; correct ChannelCount/DataWidth code tables to the silicon enums; self-enable the CKEN bus+clk gate and CMU sync in configure; and regenerate the PAC mode/i2s_crg from the vendor regs_def.

audits.driver: timer (hisi-riscv-hal: src/timer.rs, src/time.rs) — WS63 / BS2X TIMER_V150

surfaces.issue: Durations longer than the 32-bit tick range are SILENTLY CLAMPED to u32::MAX instead of being rejected. At the hardcoded 24 MHz that caps at ~178.96 s; a caller asking for e.g. 200 s gets ~179 s with no error or indication. The clamp is in src/timer.rs:213/223 (`if ticks64 > u32::MAX { u32::MAX } else ...`). The vendor C path explicitly REJECTS this: uapi_timer_start returns ERRCODE_INVALID_PARAM when `time_us > uapi_timer_get_max_us()` (timer.c:425). The u64 intermediate itself does NOT overflow (24e6 * 4.29e9 ~ 1e17 < u64::MAX), so the only defect is the silent saturation, not arithmetic UB.
surfaces.evidence: src/timer.rs:210-225, 256-272; vendor reject path /home/sanchuanhehe/Documents/hispark/fbb_ws63/src/drivers/drivers/driver/timer/timer.c:425 (`time_us > uapi_timer_get_max_us()` -> ERRCODE_INVALID_PARAM) and uapi_timer_get_max_us at timer.c:416-419

surfaces.issue: A load count of 0 compiles and is written verbatim to TIMER%s_LOAD_COUNT, then the channel is enabled. On the TIMER_V150 down-counter a load of 0 does NOT produce a zero-length delay: it either fires the interrupt immediately on the first tick or wraps a full 2^32 cycles, depending on silicon — i.e. NO usable/working output. The blocking API has no guard; only the async path defends with `ticks.max(1)` (src/timer.rs:467), so blocking callers and `start_micros(0)`/`delay_us(0)` are exposed. The vendor explicitly rejects zero: uapi_timer_start returns ERRCODE_INVALID_PARAM when `time_us == 0` (timer.c:425).
surfaces.evidence: src/timer.rs:46-61 (configure writes load_value raw), 201-204 (start), 287-289 (periodic start); async guard at src/timer.rs:467 `ticks.max(1)`; vendor zero-reject /home/sanchuanhehe/Documents/hispark/fbb_ws63/src/drivers/drivers/driver/timer/timer.c:425

surfaces.issue: Sub-tick ns requests truncate to 0 ticks. In the blocking impl (src/timer.rs:255-262) ns below one tick (<~41.7 ns at 24 MHz) compute `ticks == 0` and the `if ticks > 0` guard makes delay_ns a SILENT NO-OP (returns immediately, far shorter than requested). In the async impl (src/timer.rs:492-495) the truncated 0 is rescued by `ticks.max(1)` in delay_ticks, so the two paths disagree. Either way the result is silently wrong vs requested, not signalled.
surfaces.evidence: src/timer.rs:255-262 (blocking: `if ticks > 0` skip) vs src/timer.rs:492-495 + 467 (`ticks.max(1)`); one tick = 1/24MHz ~= 41.7 ns per the driver's own doc comment src/timer.rs:200

surfaces.field: timer counting-clock frequency assumed = 24 MHz (WS63) / 32 MHz (BS21)
surfaces.issue: All us/ms/ns->ticks conversions use the compile-time constant TIMER_CLOCK_HZ, but the WS63 timer actually counts at the board TCXO crystal, which the vendor selects at RUNTIME: 24 MHz on a CLK24M_TCXO board, else 40 MHz (clock_init.c:147-160 calls timer_porting_clock_value_set(REQ_24M) vs REQ_40M based on get_tcxo_freq()). On a 40 MHz crystal every delay produced by this driver is ~1.67x too long, and start_micros's clamp ceiling shifts — a structurally valid, compiling call yields the wrong physical time with no error. This is an UNENFORCED board precondition (the crystal must be 24 MHz for the constant to be correct). On BS2X the same constant (32 MHz) is itself flagged unconfirmed in-tree.
surfaces.evidence: src/soc/ws63.rs:48/56 TCXO_HZ=24_000_000, TIMER_CLOCK_HZ=TCXO_HZ; runtime crystal selection /home/sanchuanhehe/Documents/hispark/fbb_ws63/src/application/ws63/ws63_liteos_application/clock_init.c:147-160 (REQ_24M vs REQ_40M); vendor generic default CONFIG_TIMER_CLOCK_VALUE=32000000 (ws63_doxygen.config:410); BS21 src/soc/bs21.rs:28-32 (`TODO(bs21): confirm the exact crystal (32 MHz assumed)`)

surfaces.field: control register write (mode bits 1:2; side-effect on int_mask bit 3 / enable bit 0)
surfaces.issue: configure does a BLIND FULL WRITE `w.bits(((mode as u32) & 0x3) << 1)` (src/timer.rs:54-60), not a read-modify-write. This zeroes every other control bit, leaving int_mask (bit 3) = 0 = INTERRUPT UNMASKED on every configure. The vendor always uses RMW set_mode (hal_timer_v150_regs_op.h:67-73) to preserve int_mask. Not an overflow (the TimerMode enum only yields 0/1, well inside the 2-bit field, so class A does not apply), but the implicit unmasking is an unenforced side-effect: a blocking OneShot user who never routes the TIMER IRQ gets a pending+unmasked line; correctness depends on the caller's interrupt setup. Lower severity because it is consistent and the async path relies on it.
surfaces.evidence: src/timer.rs:54-60 (full `w.bits(...)` write, no RMW); PAC int_mask is control bit 3 (lib.rs timer_control IntMaskW new(self,3)); vendor RMW set_mode /home/sanchuanhehe/Documents/hispark/fbb_ws63/src/drivers/drivers/hal/timer/v150/hal_timer_v150_regs_op.h:67-73

surfaces.field: mode = Periodic (HW periodic reload) — unverified on silicon
surfaces.issue: The driver programs control.mode = 1 (PERIODIC) and trusts the hardware to auto-reload. The vendor C SDK NEVER programs HW periodic mode: hal_timer_v150_config_load always forces TIMER_V150_MODE_ONE_SHOT (hal_timer_v150.c:80) and implements periodic behaviour entirely in software (the soft-timer manager in driver/timer.c reloads on each IRQ). This means the Rust PeriodicTimer's reliance on mode=1 auto-reload is NOT exercised by the silicon-validated vendor path and may behave differently on real silicon (e.g. mode=1 may still need the cnt/load handshake or may not reload as assumed) — needs on-board confirmation before being trusted. Structurally valid and compiles, possibly unrunnable-as-intended.
surfaces.evidence: src/timer.rs:285-290 + 46-61; vendor forces one-shot + software periodic: /home/sanchuanhehe/Documents/hispark/fbb_ws63/src/drivers/drivers/hal/timer/v150/hal_timer_v150.c:78-87 (config_load always TIMER_V150_MODE_ONE_SHOT) and software reload in driver/timer.c:170-185

audits.notes:
SAFE FIELDS (verified, no issue invented): (1) load_value:u32 is a genuinely full-width 32-bit register — PAC LoadCountW = FieldWriter<32,u32> (ws63-pac lib.rs:23437) and vendor load_count0 is uint32 with CONFIG_TIMER_0_WIDTH_64=0 on WS63 (ws63_doxygen.config:417), so no class-A truncation. (2) The mode field is a 2-bit field and TimerMode only encodes 0/1, so `(mode as u32)&0x3` cannot overflow into adjacent bits — no class A. (3) us->ticks multiply uses u64 and cannot overflow u64 for any u32 us, so the only issue is the saturating clamp, not arithmetic UB (the existing proptests confirm this).

CLOCK GATING: unlike PWM, the TIMER block has NO per-peripheral CKEN/CRG gate in the vendor porting layer (timer_porting.c has no clock-enable sequence; it only sets a *software* clock-VALUE for us<->cycle math via timer_porting_clock_value_set). The timer counts off the always-on TCXO/system crystal the bootloader already brought up, so 'construct -> clocked' holds WITHOUT the driver self-enabling a gate. Therefore the PWM-style class-C 'missing clock gate' does NOT apply here; the class-C exposure is instead the wrong/unconfirmed crystal FREQUENCY constant (see high-severity row).

CHIP GATING: both chips share identical TIMER_V150 field widths (BS2X PAC lib.rs:4418 LoadCountW<32,u32>, :4544 ModeW<...,2>). WS63 timer crystal is runtime 24 MHz-or-40 MHz; BS21 TIMER_CLOCK_HZ=32 MHz is self-flagged TODO/unconfirmed in soc/bs21.rs:28. Both frequency assumptions need on-board confirmation.

HIGHEST-VALUE FIXES (emulating the PWM newtype pattern): introduce a construct-time-validated TimerTicks(u32) newtype with from_ticks (reject 0), try_from_micros/try_from_millis (reject 0 and >max, return Option instead of clamping), making configure/start take TimerTicks so zero-period and over-range become unrepresentable; and either make TIMER_CLOCK_HZ a runtime-read of get_tcxo_freq() or type the crystal so a 40 MHz board cannot silently use the 24 MHz constant. The current/value latch handshake (cnt_req/cnt_lock) is already correctly implemented (matches vendor hal_timer_v150_get_current_value) — not a config defect.

UNCERTAIN / NEEDS BOARD CONFIRMATION: (a) whether HW mode=1 (periodic) actually auto-reloads on silicon, since the vendor never uses it; (b) exact WS63 crystal of the target board (24 vs 40 MHz); (c) BS21 actual TCXO frequency.

surfaces.issue: INVERTED polarity vs the vendor SDK. detect() returns MHz24 when (hw_ctl & 0x1) == 0 and MHz40 otherwise. The vendor get_tcxo_freq does the opposite: bit0==1 -> 24 MHz, bit0==0 -> 40 MHz. So on a 40 MHz board (bit0=0) detect() reports MHz24, and on a 24 MHz board it reports MHz40. This is a compiles-and-runs-but-wrong value: the wrong TcxoFreq propagates into SystemClocks.tcxo_freq, the cpu_clk/pclk fallback used when the PLL is NOT locked (returns 40M instead of 24M and vice versa), and every busy-wait cycle count in wait_pll_lock and init_clocks step 1 (a ~1.67x delay error). The struct doc table (clock_init.rs:53) 'bit[0]: 0=24MHz, 1=40MHz' is itself backwards. The HAL's OWN uart_boot_clock_hz (soc/ws63.rs:97) gets it right (bit0==1 -> 24M), so the two readers of the same strap disagree.
surfaces.evidence: fbb_ws63 soc_porting.h:22-23 (CLK24M_TCXO=1, CLK40M_TCXO=0) + soc_porting.c:23 ((hw_ctl & 0x1)==CLK24M_TCXO -> 24M). HAL self-contradiction: soc/ws63.rs:97 vs clock_init.rs:106. Silicon note (memory ws63-hal-silicon-bringup / soc/ws63.rs:83): a 40 MHz board reads HW_CTL=0x2 (bit0 clear), confirming bit0=0 == 40 MHz.

surfaces.issue: init_clocks switches the flash controller (step 1, CLK_SEL bit18), UART0/1/2 (step 2, bits 1/2/3) and SPI (step 3, bit 6) source muxes to PLL, and ONLY THEN (step 4) polls REG_EXCEP_RO_RG bit 12 for PLL lock. If the PLL is not actually locked (non-flashboot bring-up, hardware fault), every switched domain is now muxed to a dead/unstable clock. Worst case the flash controller it may be executing from gets a non-running clock and the core hangs before step 4 ever returns pll_locked=false. The function returns a SystemClocks whose pll_locked=false is structurally valid but by then the damage (sources already on a dead PLL) is done. The vendor never re-switches flash post-boot from the app, and brings the RF/PLL reference up (open_rf_power, DBG_CMU_XO_PD=ONEN_RF_PLL) BEFORE switching sources.
surfaces.evidence: clock_init.rs:202-245 (switch-then-verify order). Vendor switch_clock (clock_init.c:99) sets DBG_CMU_XO_PD=ONEN_RF_PLL and delays before any source switch; flashboot calls switch_flash_clock_to_pll only after sfc_flash_init while running from SRAM (flashboot main.c:98).

surfaces.issue: Step 1 re-runs the vendor switch_flash_clock_to_pll sequence (CMU_NEW_CFG1 0x1 -> 0x3, CLK_SEL bit18) on the live flash/SFC controller. The vendor only does this inside flashboot, which executes from SRAM, immediately after sfc_flash_init. A bare-metal Rust app that is executing-in-place (XIP) from SPI flash will reconfigure the very controller fetching its instructions; mid-sequence the fetch clock can glitch and the core hangs. The signature takes &SysCtl0 and &CldoCrg but ignores both (_sys_ctl0/_cldo_crg) and writes raw MMIO, so it neither requires nor proves the caller is running from SRAM, nor takes exclusive ownership of the CRG (other live CldoCrg references can race it). The XIP-must-be-off precondition is documented in prose only (lib.rs / soc/ws63.rs:66 'XIP-unsafe full clock_init') and not encoded in the type.
surfaces.evidence: fbb_ws63 soc_porting.c:56-61 (switch_flash_clock_to_pll), flashboot main.c:98 calls it from SRAM after sfc_flash_init; soc/ws63.rs:66 documents 'the (XIP-unsafe) full clock_init'. Rust args _sys_ctl0/_cldo_crg are unused (clock_init.rs:197).

surfaces.issue: init_clocks performs NO PLL power-up or configuration — it only switches source muxes and polls lock. The doc claims 'Boot ROM configures the CPU PLL before loading the application.' For any path where that is not true (non-flashboot bring-up, cold custom loader), the call structurally succeeds, returns pll_locked=false and cpu_clk=tcxo, yet has already pointed flash/UART/SPI at the unpowered PLL (see ordering defect). The reference vendor flow brings the RF/analog PLL up explicitly (open_rf_power + DBG_CMU_XO_PD ONEN_RF_PLL). This is an unenforced 'boot ROM already locked the PLL' precondition.
surfaces.evidence: clock_init.rs:166-169 (SystemClocks::assumed 'assumes boot ROM has configured PLL'), :17-18 module doc 'CPU PLL configured by the boot ROM'. Vendor open_rf_power / switch_clock (clock_init.c:59-138) does the PLL/RF reference enable the Rust port omits.

surfaces.field: missing TRNG / TSENSOR / I2C / Timer / WDT clock setup that the vendor clock_init does
surfaces.issue: init_clocks stops after switching flash/UART/SPI to PLL. The vendor switch_clock ALSO programs the TCXO-frequency-dependent TRNG divider (CLDO_CRG_DIV_CTL9[8:6], div 2 for 24M / div 4 for 40M) and TSENSOR divider (CLDO_CRG_DIV_CTL7[11:6], 0x18 for 24M / 0x28 for 40M), and set_uart_tcxo_clock_period sets the I2C, timer and WDT counting clocks to the detected TCXO value. A caller who runs init_clocks and then constructs Trng/Tsensor/I2c/Timer/Watchdog drivers gets the reset-default (or wrong-TCXO) divider — structurally valid construction, but TRNG/TSENSOR sample at the wrong rate and I2C/timer/WDT tick math is wrong on a 40 MHz board because no per-TCXO divider was applied. clock.rs::cken_info returns None for exactly these (TRNG/Tsensor/Timer/I2c), reinforcing the 'default-on, nobody configures them' gap.
surfaces.evidence: fbb_ws63 clock_init.c:119-138 (TRNG DIV_CTL9, TSENSOR DIV_CTL7 per-TCXO) and clock_init.c:142-166 (set_uart_tcxo_clock_period sets I2C/timer/WDT to REQ_24M/REQ_40M). None of these registers are touched by init_clocks (clock_init.rs:216-253).

surfaces.issue: cken_info returns None for 11 peripherals to mean 'the SDK does not individually gate it; rely on the reset-default clock.' This encodes an unenforced precondition: it is only true if the boot ROM/flashboot left those gates enabled. A consumer (safety.rs drift checks, or future gating code) reading None cannot distinguish 'no gate exists' from 'gate exists but we did not map it.' Notably Spi1 returns None while Spi0 returns Some((1,25)); SPI1 shares the same SPI clock domain that the SPI driver self-enables, but there is no recorded gate for a code path that wanted to gate SPI1 independently. For a driver that did want to gate-then-use one of these, None gives it no bit and it would silently operate on whatever the reset/boot state left — works on a flashboot board, may not on a custom loader. This is a reference-table precondition, not a register-overflow.
surfaces.evidence: clock.rs:68-79 + module doc clock.rs:14-18. Vendor does gate SFC (CKEN bits) and the DMA/SDMA/security blocks elsewhere; the table intentionally omits unverified bits. Spi0 Some((1,25)) confirmed (spi_porting.c:697); Spi1 unmapped.

surfaces.issue: assumed() is a const fabricator that hard-codes tcxo_freq=MHz40 and pll_locked=true without reading hardware. On a 24 MHz board, code that builds clocks via assumed() (instead of probe_clocks) gets tcxo_freq=MHz40 — a structurally valid but wrong value that throws off any TCXO-derived timing (timer/WDT/I2C tick math) by 40/24. It silently substitutes a guess for a measurement; nothing signals the mismatch.
surfaces.evidence: clock_init.rs:167-169 hard-codes MHz40/true. The real board may be 24 MHz (soc/ws63.rs:35 'TCXO 24 MHz on standard WS63 modules', TCXO_HZ=24_000_000). Contrast probe_clocks (clock_init.rs:260) which actually reads the strap.

audits.notes:
Scope reality: clock.rs and clock_init.rs are mostly probe/reference code, not a numeric Config-struct surface, so there are NO classic register-field-overflow (class A) defects here — init_clocks writes whole 32-bit words via raw pointers (read-modify-write of single bits), not narrow PAC fields, so there is nothing to silently truncate. The defects are semantic/precondition (B/C/D).

HIGHEST-CONFIDENCE finding (needs no board to confirm, it is a source contradiction): TcxoFreq::detect (clock_init.rs:106) inverts the TCXO strap polarity relative to BOTH the vendor SDK (soc_porting.h CLK24M_TCXO=1/CLK40M_TCXO=0) AND the HAL's own uart_boot_clock_hz (soc/ws63.rs:97). The two HAL readers of HW_CTL bit0 disagree; the silicon note (40 MHz board reads HW_CTL=0x2, bit0 clear) sides with uart_boot_clock_hz, making detect() the wrong one. This is worth on-board confirmation but the static evidence is already strong.

The 'self-enable-gate theme for other drivers' the prompt referenced is implemented in the peripheral drivers, not in clock.rs: spi.rs::configure_spi_source_clock (DIV_CTL3 5-bit field, clamped to 0x1F correctly per SPI_DIV_LEN=5; CLK_SEL bit6; CKEN1 bit25) is the SPI analogue of PWM's self-clocking, and it carries its own unenforced precondition ('Requires the PLL to already be locked', spi.rs:109) — same class-C theme. PWM's CKEN_CTL0[10:2] base bit (2) is correctly recorded. These live outside the two audited files; flag for the SPI/PWM audits.

Clock gating chip-gating: the whole clock.rs/clock_init.rs module is #[cfg(feature = "chip-ws63")] (lib.rs:66-69); BS2X has a different CRG layout (64 MHz app core) and does NOT use these. All register addresses (0x4400_11xx CLDO_CRG, 0x4000_xxxx CMU) are WS63-physical.

Unverified-on-silicon items needing a board: (1) detect() polarity — confirm a known-24MHz board makes detect() return MHz40 (the bug); (2) whether init_clocks actually hangs under XIP — the prose says XIP-unsafe but it has not been run from flash; examples deliberately skip it (hisi-riscv-rs#10/#15).

audits.driver: i2c (WS63 i2c.rs custom v150 + BS2X i2c_v151.rs DesignWare)

surfaces.issue: half = pclk/(4*freq) = 6_000_000/freq is written with raw w.bits(half) into a 16-bit field, but for any requested freq <= 91 Hz the value exceeds 0xFFFF and silicon ignores bits 16:31 (field is bits 0:15), so the programmed value is half & 0xFFFF — a garbage, far-too-fast SCL. e.g. freq=1 -> half=6_000_000 -> programmed 36224; freq=50 -> half=120000 -> programmed 54464. Compiles and looks valid; bus runs at a wildly wrong rate, no error.
surfaces.evidence: ws63-pac/src/lib.rs:8659 SclHW=FieldWriter<...,16,u16> + :8704 scl_l masks &0xffff (bits 0:15); vendor hal_i2c_v150_regs_def.h:292/313 `scl_h:16`/`scl_l:16`. Driver writes raw full-u32 w.bits(half) (i2c.rs:46-47) via generic W::bits which stores the whole REG::Ux=u32. Overflow boundary freq<6_000_000/65535≈91.6Hz.

surfaces.issue: For freq >= 6_000_001 Hz, half = 6_000_000/freq = 0, so both SCL high and low period registers are programmed to 0 -> zero-length clock phases -> no working SCL / dead bus. Any freq from ~6 MHz up to u32::MAX is accepted and silently yields a non-functional controller. The vendor hard-rejects baudrate above the 3.4 MHz HS limit; this driver has no upper bound at all.
surfaces.evidence: half==0 for freq>6e6 (pclk=24MHz, half=pclk/(4*freq)). Vendor caps baudrate at I2C_HS_MODE_BAUDRATE_HIGH_LIMIT=3400*1000 and returns ERRCODE_I2C_RATE_INVALID otherwise: drivers/drivers/driver/i2c/i2c.c:933 (`baudrate > I2C_HS_MODE_BAUDRATE_HIGH_LIMIT`), limit in hal/i2c/hal_i2c.h:32.

surfaces.issue: `let freq = if freq == 0 { 1 } else { freq };` silently clamps a 0-Hz request to 1 Hz instead of signalling. 1 Hz then hits the worst-case overflow path (half=6_000_000 -> truncated to 36224), so a caller bug (freq=0) is converted into a silently-misconfigured bus rather than an error or a sane default. The vendor explicitly rejects baudrate==0.
surfaces.evidence: i2c.rs:43 zero-guard. Vendor i2c_init_check rejects baudrate==0 with ERRCODE_I2C_RATE_INVALID: drivers/drivers/driver/i2c/i2c.c:933 (`|| baudrate == 0`), and again at i2c.c:1284.

surfaces.issue: The constructor takes a bare u32 freq with an infallible signature (returns Self, not Option/Result), so the entire unrunnable band (freq<=91 Hz truncates, freq>=6 MHz dead) is reachable with zero feedback. The reference PWM pattern uses a construct-time-validated newtype (from_count/try_from_hz -> Option) so an out-of-range value is unrepresentable. I2c::new takes a raw integer instead, so 'compiles and constructs' does NOT imply 'programmable'.
surfaces.evidence: i2c.rs:27/34 `pub fn new_i2c0(_i2c, freq: u32) -> Self`. Valid HW band is roughly 92 Hz..5.99 MHz for pclk=24MHz; vendor's enforced band is 1 Hz..3.4 MHz with explicit error returns (i2c.c:933). Contrast PWM newtype pattern in task brief.

surfaces.field: pclk source-clock constant (24 MHz) used in the divider
surfaces.issue: The SCL divider is computed against I2C_CLOCK_HZ = TCXO 24 MHz, but this constant is unverified and contradicted within the same codebase: the vendor porting default for non-FPGA silicon is 40 MHz, and the HAL's own clock_init table lists I2C at 80 MHz PLL-derived. If the true I2C clock is 40 MHz (or 80 MHz), every computed half-period is too small by ~1.67x (or ~3.3x), so a requested 100 kHz actually runs at ~167 kHz (or ~333 kHz) — silently out of I2C spec. Needs on-board confirmation of the real I2C clock.
surfaces.evidence: soc/ws63.rs:117 I2C_CLOCK_HZ=TCXO_HZ=24_000_000. Vendor porting default = BUS_CLOCK_TIME_40M for non-FPGA: drivers/chips/ws63/porting/i2c/i2c_porting.c:19-26,47-48 (g_i2c_clock initialised to I2C_CLOCK_DEFAULT=40MHz). HAL doc contradicts: clock_init.rs:68 lists I2C=80 MHz PLL-derived. Three different values across the stack.

surfaces.field: scl_h / scl_l computed value (vs vendor cfg_clk)
surfaces.issue: Driver programs half = period/2 directly into both scl_h and scl_l. Vendor cfg_clk computes clk_val = (i2c_clk/(baudrate*2))*scl_h/(scl_l+scl_h) - 1 and the symmetric form for scl_l, i.e. it subtracts 1 (counter is N+1 cycles) and applies the configured high/low weighting. Omitting the -1 makes SCL slightly faster than requested; for small divisor values (high freq, e.g. freq=3.4MHz -> half=1) the missing -1 is a large relative error. Not a truncation, but a silent rate skew vs the authoritative formula.
surfaces.evidence: Vendor hal_i2c_v150_cfg_clk: drivers/drivers/hal/i2c/v150/hal_i2c_v150_comm.c:350-353 (`(i2c_clk/(baudrate*2))*scl_h/(scl_l+scl_h) - 1`). Rust omits the -1 and the scl_h/(scl_h+scl_l) weighting (i2c.rs:44-47).

surfaces.field: scl_params hardcoded SCL counts (160/190 SS, 40/50 FS)
surfaces.issue: The SCL high/low counts are fixed magic numbers chosen for a 'nominal ~32 MHz' I2C clock and are NOT derived from soc::chip::I2C_CLOCK_HZ (the module's own TODO admits this). They write cleanly into the 16-bit cnt fields (no overflow — values fit), so this is not a truncation bug; but if the real BS2X I2C clock differs from 32 MHz the produced 100k/400k rates are wrong on silicon, with no way for a caller to correct them. Construct-time-valid but rate-unverified.
surfaces.evidence: i2c_v151.rs:77-82 scl_params returns fixed (1,160,190)/(2,40,50); :75-76 explicit TODO 'derive from soc::chip::I2C_CLOCK_HZ per hal_i2c_v151 scl-count formula', comment 'exact divisor only matters on silicon (QEMU model ignores it)'. bs2x soc I2C_CLOCK_HZ=TCXO_HZ=32_000_000 (soc/bs21.rs:29,41). Field cnt width=16 (bs2x-pac/src/lib.rs:6296) — counts fit, so the defect is wrong-rate, not overflow.

surfaces.method: BS2X Speed enum (i2c_v151.rs:18-23) — no High-Speed / arbitrary rate
surfaces.issue: Speed only exposes Standard (100k) and Fast (400k). IC_CON.speed=3 (HS) and any rate between/below are unreachable. This is a feature-coverage limitation rather than a silent-unrunnable input (the two exposed values ARE programmable), so it is the least severe item — noted for completeness, not a truncation/clamp bug.
surfaces.evidence: i2c_v151.rs:18-23 enum Speed { Standard, Fast }; scl_params only maps these two (i2c_v151.rs:77-82). DesignWare IC_CON.speed supports 1=SS/2=FS/3=HS per the module doc comment (i2c_v151.rs:74-75).

audits.notes:
Clock gating: WS63 I2C has NO individual clock gate in the HAL (clock.rs:69-79 maps I2c0/I2c1 to None = 'default-on, no authoritative bit'), and the vendor only calls i2c_port_clock_enable under CONFIG_I2C_SUPPORT_LPC (i2c.c:963-965), so the TCXO-derived I2C clock is assumed always-on — there is no missing gate-enable (no class-C gating defect of the PWM kind). configure_i2c does NOT self-enable a clock tree, which is acceptable IF the I2C clock is truly default-on; needs on-board confirmation.

PRIMARY uncertainty needing silicon confirmation: the actual WS63 I2C source clock. Three values coexist in-tree — 24 MHz (driver constant soc/ws63.rs:117), 40 MHz (vendor porting default i2c_porting.c:25), 80 MHz (HAL doc clock_init.rs:68). The 16-bit overflow boundaries I computed assume 24 MHz; if it is 40/80 MHz the low-freq overflow boundary shifts (overflow for freq below ~152 Hz at 40 MHz, ~305 Hz at 80 MHz) and the dead-zone upper bound rises to 10/20 MHz, but the SHAPE of every defect (low-freq truncation, high-freq half=0, freq==0 clamp, no range check) is independent of which value is correct. The field is genuinely 16-bit on both PAC and vendor regs_def, so unlike PWM's high-half-doesn't-latch surprise the truncation is well-attested by the register map, not just measured.

Suggested fix shape (mirrors PWM): replace the bare `freq: u32` with a construct-time-validated newtype, e.g. I2cFreq(u16-derived) whose try_from_hz(pclk, hz) returns None when the resulting scl_h/scl_l would be 0 or exceed 0xFFFF (equivalently when hz is outside ~[pclk/(4*65535), pclk/4]); also enforce the vendor's <=3.4MHz / !=0 bounds. BS2X already uses an enum (safe by construction) but should derive its SCL counts from I2C_CLOCK_HZ.

BS2X v151 vendor source (fbb_bs2x hal_i2c_v151) is NOT present in this checkout (only fbb_ws63 is), so the BS2X SCL-count correctness could not be cross-checked against vendor ground truth — flagged as class-C unverified, consistent with the module's own TODO.

surfaces.issue: steal(pin) computes block = pin/8, bit = pin%8 with NO validation. Valid WS63 pins are 0..=18 (GPIO0 bits0-7, GPIO1 bits8-15→bits0-7, GPIO2 only pins16-18 = block2 bits0-2). steal(19..=23) yields block 2 bits 3..7 — registers exist (sw_oen is a full 8-bit field, masks &0xff) and the write succeeds silently, but GPIO2 only has 3 physical pads (vendor gpio_porting.c: GPIO_CHANNEL_2 = GPIO_16~GPIO_18, pin_num=3), so the pin does nothing on silicon. steal(24..) → block>=3 → regs() match hits unreachable!() and panics at runtime. The doc says 'must be 0-18' but nothing enforces it.
surfaces.evidence: gpio_sw_oen is FieldWriter<8> masked &0xff (ws63-pac lib.rs ~line 5449/5455 — writable for all 8 bits of every block); vendor gpio_porting.c GPIO_CHANNEL_2 covers GPIO_16~GPIO_18 only (pin_num=3); regs() in gpio.rs:387 has `_ => unreachable!()` for block>=3

surfaces.issue: Using a pad as GPIO REQUIRES the per-pad mux sel register (gpio_NN_sel / uartX_*_sel) to select the GPIO function first. The GPIO driver never touches the mux — it only writes gpio_sw_oen / gpio_data_*. If a pad's mux defaults to or was left on a peripheral alt-function (PWM/SPI/JTAG/UART/DIAG per the *_sel doc strings), the GPIO data/oen writes have no effect on the pad and the pin produces no output / reads garbage. Vendor always calls uapi_pin_set_mode(pin, HAL_PIO_FUNC_GPIO) before gpio dir/val.
surfaces.evidence: vendor blinky_demo.c:24 `uapi_pin_set_mode(CONFIG_BLINKY_PIN, HAL_PIO_FUNC_GPIO)` then :26 set_dir / :27 set_val; radar_sta_sample.c:81 `uapi_pin_set_mode(GPIO_13, PIN_MODE_0)`; ws63-pac gpio_00_sel doc '0:GPIO_00; 1:PWM0; 2:DIAG[0]; 3:SPI1_CSN; 4:JTAG_TDI' (non-zero = alt function)

surfaces.method: AnyPin::init_input -> apply_pull (gpio.rs:423) and IoConfigDriver methods (io_config.rs) on chip-bs21
surfaces.issue: The `gpio` module is NOT chip-gated (lib.rs:42 `pub mod gpio;`), so on chip-bs21 (BS2X) init_input()→apply_pull() does a raw write_volatile to 0x4400_D000 + 0x800 + pin*4. BS2X has no IoConfig peripheral and no pad_gpio_NN_ctrl/gpio_NN_sel registers at all — that address is not the BS2X pin-control block. The pull/IE write lands on a wrong/unmapped address; pull + input-buffer enable silently do not take effect on BS2X (and may corrupt an unrelated register). The module doc even notes 'BS21's IO_CONFIG differs — ported later'. io_config.rs IS chip-ws63-gated, but gpio.rs's apply_pull duplicates the WS63 address inline and is reachable on BS2X.
surfaces.evidence: bs2x-pac lib.rs: Gpio0/1/2 at 0x5701_0000/4000/8000 (not WS63's 0x4402_8xxx); grep for 0x4400_D000 / IoConfig / gpio_NN_sel / pad_gpio_NN_ctrl in bs2x-pac → 0 matches; gpio.rs:404 `const IO_CONFIG_BASE: usize = 0x4400_D000` is unconditional; io_config gated #[cfg(feature="chip-ws63")] at lib.rs:92 but gpio not gated

surfaces.field: function: u8 masked with 0x07 written to a 2-bit field
surfaces.issue: set_uart_mux masks `function & 0x07` (3 bits) and writes via w.bits(val) to the uartX_*_sel register, but the hardware sel field for UART pads is only 2 bits wide (values 0..=3). A caller passing function 4..=7 has bit 2 written into a reserved/adjacent register bit instead of being rejected — silent out-of-range. (set_gpio_mux's 0x07 mask is correct: GPIO sel fields ARE 3-bit.)
surfaces.evidence: ws63-pac uart0_txd_sel: Uart0TxdSelW = FieldWriter<REG, 2>, reader masks `self.bits & 3`; vendor hal_pinctrl_ws63.c HAL_PIN_UART_SEL_BITS_NUM=2 vs HAL_PIN_GPIO_SEL_BITS_NUM=3; io_config.rs:194 `let val = (function & 0x07) as u32`

surfaces.field: whole pad-ctrl register (full .write overwrites mode/ST/IE/DS bits not in build_pad_ctrl)
surfaces.issue: These do an unconditional full-register `.write(|w| w.bits(build_pad_ctrl(...)))`, zeroing every bit not explicitly set. build_pad_ctrl only sets DS[6:4], PE/PS[10:9], ST[3], IE[11]; bits 0-2, 7-8, and >=12 are forced to 0. Critically, calling configure_gpio_pad with input_enable=false clears IE (bit11) for that pad, which disables the input buffer — a subsequent GPIO read returns 0 regardless of pin state. The vendor never does a full write: every field is a read-modify-write (reg32_setbits masks only the target field), so unrelated bits are preserved. A construct-time-valid 'output pad' config silently disables input on that pad.
surfaces.evidence: vendor reg32_setbits (chip_io.h:265) is RMW of only [pos, pos+bits-1]; hal_pin_set_config_val writes each config type (mode/ds/pull/ie/st) independently; io_config.rs build_pad_ctrl (366) only ORs DS/PE/PS/ST/IE — all other bits 0; configure_gpio_pad uses .write() not .modify()

surfaces.field: ULP 32K clock precondition for interrupt; module not chip-gated
surfaces.issue: ULP_GPIO interrupt operation needs the ULP GPIO clock set to 32K (vendor gpio_ulp_int_en: 'Enable ulp gpio interrupt, set ulp gpio clk as 32K'). UlpGpioPin::enable_interrupt only sets gpio_int_en — it never provisions the 32K clock, so an armed ULP interrupt may never fire on silicon (unenforced clock precondition; the driver does not self-enable its clock tree the way PWM does). Secondary: ulp_gpio is NOT chip-gated, and UlpGpio resolves to 0x5703_0000 on BOTH chips via the PAC, but the ULP clock/IE provisioning path is WS63-validated only; on BS2X this is unconfirmed.
surfaces.evidence: vendor gpio_porting.h:102-105 `gpio_ulp_int_en(bool on)` doc 'set ulp gpio clk as 32K' (body is a stub on WS63 porting → clock handled elsewhere/by ROM; not provisioned by this Rust driver); ulp_gpio.rs enable_interrupt only touches gpio_int_en; lib.rs:156 `pub mod ulp_gpio;` ungated

surfaces.field: pin: u8 -> bit = pin - 107 (underflow for pin < 107)
surfaces.issue: bit is computed as `pin - 107` BEFORE the `assert!(bit < 8)` check. In release builds (overflow checks off, the norm for embedded) any pin < 107 wraps the u8 subtraction (e.g. pin=106 → bit=255), and 255 < 8 is false so the assert still catches it — but pin=99 → 99-107 wraps to 248, also caught; the wrap is benign only because the post-wrap value stays >= 8. It is fragile (relies on wrap landing >=8) and the assert is a runtime panic rather than a fallible Option/Result, unlike the PWM reference pattern. No silent-unrunnable value slips through here, but the surface is a panic-on-bad-input construction rather than a typed/fallible one.
surfaces.evidence: ulp_gpio.rs:143 `let bit = pin - 107;` then :144 `assert!(bit < 8, ...)`; valid range GPIO107..=114 (8 pins); contrast PWM newtype from_* returning Option instead of asserting

surfaces.field: pin: u8 (no bound; block = pin/8 into regs())
surfaces.issue: Same class as steal but for the legacy free functions: no validation of pin. pin 19..=23 → block 2 bits 3..7 silently no-op on silicon (only 3 pads in GPIO2); pin >= 24 → block >= 3 → regs() unreachable!() panic. These are public functions taking a bare u8 with a doc claim '0-18' that is unenforced.
surfaces.evidence: gpio.rs:550 `let block = pin / 8;` with no range check; regs() block>=3 → unreachable!(); vendor GPIO_CHANNEL_2 = 3 pins (gpio_porting.c)

audits.notes:
Verified-SAFE (NOT defects, do not 'fix'): (1) Pull encoding in apply_pull and build_pad_ctrl is CORRECT — Up=PE+PS={bit10,bit9}=0b11, Down=PE only=0b01 — matching vendor pin_pull_t (1=PULL_DOWN, 3=PULL_UP) and the WS63 2-bit pull field at HAL_PIN_PULL_START_BIT=9, BITS_NUM=2. Note the ws63-pac doc string ('01:strong pull-up; 10:pull-down') is the textbook DesignWare convention and is MISLEADING for this part — the vendor SDK + silicon use {PS,PE}: 1=down, 2=strong-up, 3=up. The Rust matches the vendor, not the PAC doc. (2) DriveStrength enum (0..=7) exactly fills the 3-bit DS[6:4] field — no overflow possible. (3) set_gpio_mux 0x07 mask matches the 3-bit GPIO sel field. (4) InterruptTrigger / Pull / PinMux / SfcPad enums can only hold valid values. (5) GPIO data/oen register writes are per-bit (1<<bit) and structurally fine.

Missing-feature (not a defect, but worth noting): the Rust Pull enum cannot express vendor value 2 = PIN_PULL_TYPE_STRONG_UP (strong pull-up); only None/Down/Up(weak) are reachable.

NEEDS ON-BOARD CONFIRMATION: (a) the BS2X (chip-bs21) GPIO + ULP path — gpio.rs and ulp_gpio.rs are ungated and reuse WS63 assumptions (apply_pull's 0x4400_D000 IO_CONFIG address does not exist on BS2X; BS2X has zero gpio_NN_sel/pad_gpio_NN_ctrl regs). Whether a BS2X GPIO works at all without the BS2X-specific pinmux block is unconfirmed and is the highest-value thing to check on a BS21 board. (b) the mux precondition: confirm whether the WS63 reset-default mux for the GPIO-capable pads is the GPIO function (sel=0) — if reset default is already GPIO for the pads blinky uses, the missing set_gpio_mux only bites pins whose ROM/bootloader left them on an alt function; the silicon-validated Rust blinky must be setting the mux via IoConfigDriver out-of-band. (c) ULP interrupt 32K clock: confirm whether ROM leaves the ULP clock running (WS63 gpio_ulp_int_en is a stub) or whether enable_interrupt silently never fires.

surfaces.issue: The SpiIfType discriminants are cast straight into the 3-bit rd_mem_if_type[0:2]/wr_mem_if_type[16:18] fields, but the hardware mem_if_type encoding is NOT a dense 0..4. The valid encodings are {0=Standard, 1=Dual-Output, 2=Dual-IO, 5=Quad-Output, 6=Quad-IO}; encodings 3 and 4 are RESERVED. So SpiIfType::QuadIO writes 3 and SpiIfType::QuadIOCont writes 4 -- both reserved -- and the controller drives no working quad output (silently degrades to a reserved/undefined interface). The enum can even hold a value that compiles and looks like 'quad' but never produces a quad transfer on silicon. (Also note the labels are skewed: discriminant 1 the driver calls DualIO is actually Dual-Output in HW, and 2 is Dual-IO.)
surfaces.evidence: PAC rd_mem_if_type/wr_mem_if_type are 3-bit (ws63-pac/src/lib.rs:17186-17205, 'FieldWriter<3>', doc '000=Standard;001=Dual;010=Dual-IO;101=Quad-Input;110=Quad-IO'). Vendor authoritative mapping: fbb_ws63/src/drivers/chips/ws63/porting/sfc/flash_config/flash_config_info.c:77-93 g_flash_common_read_cmds/g_flash_common_write_cmds where the 3rd struct field is the iftype register value: 0x3B->1, 0xBB->2, 0x6B->5, 0xEB->6, quad write 0x32->5. Values 3,4 never appear.

surfaces.issue: configure_bus builds the BUS_CONFIG1 word using only bits [0:29] and does a blind full register write(|w| w.bits(cfg1)), so rd_enable (bit30) and wr_enable (bit31) are left at 0. Memory-mapped (bus/XIP) reads and writes are therefore disabled -- the bus config is structurally valid but produces no working memory-mapped access. A caller who calls configure_bus expecting XIP reads to work gets a silently non-functional bus.
surfaces.evidence: PAC BusConfig1 defines wr_enable=bit30, rd_enable=bit31 (ws63-pac/src/lib.rs:17214-17221, '#[doc = "Bit 30 - Bus write enable"]', '#[doc = "Bit 31 - Bus read enable"]'). Vendor sets them explicitly: fbb_ws63/src/drivers/drivers/hal/sfc/hal_sfc_v150_regs_op.c:72 'bus_config.b.rd_enable = RD_ENABLE;' and :85 'bus_config.b.wr_enable = WR_ENABLE;'. Driver sfc.rs:168-184 never sets bits 30/31.

surfaces.field: BUS_CONFIG1 written as blind full write instead of read-modify-write
surfaces.issue: Vendor configures read and write halves of BUS_CONFIG1 independently with read-modify-write (set_bus_read preserves the write half + enables, set_bus_write preserves the read half), and BUS_CONFIG2 (wip_locate, DDR dummy bytes) lives in a sibling register. The driver's single blind write of cfg1 clobbers rd_enable/wr_enable to 0 (see above) and assumes a one-shot config; combined with no BUS_CONFIG2 handling the bus config cannot represent the vendor's working setup. The unenforced precondition is that the bus must be quiesced/reset (release_bus_reset/hold_bus_reset) coherently before/after this write.
surfaces.evidence: fbb_ws63/src/drivers/drivers/hal/sfc/hal_sfc_v150_regs_op.c:68-87 both setters do 'bus_config.d32 = ...->bus_config1;' then OR in fields (RMW). BUS_CONFIG2 fields (wip_locate, bus_ddr_mode) in hal_sfc_v150_regs_def.h:123-135. Driver sfc.rs:181-183 does an unconditional write.

surfaces.issue: The bus_dma_len register stores n where the actual transfer length is (n+1) bytes. The vendor writes dma_len = length-1. The Rust driver writes 'length & 0x3FFF_FFFF' directly, so every DMA transfers ONE BYTE MORE than requested (e.g. length=256 transfers 257 bytes), corrupting the byte after the buffer / over-reading flash. length=0 is also accepted and wraps the field to 0x3FFF_FFFF (max), i.e. a ~1GB transfer instead of a no-op.
surfaces.evidence: PAC bus_dma_len is 30-bit, doc 'DMA length (n+1 bytes)' (ws63-pac/src/lib.rs:17533-17538). Vendor: fbb_ws63/src/drivers/drivers/hal/sfc/hal_sfc_v150_regs_op.h:114 'len_conf.b.dma_len = length - 1;'. regs_def hal_sfc_v150_regs_def.h:208 'Data length = (dma_len + 1) Byte'. Driver sfc.rs:406 writes 'length & 0x3FFF_FFFF' with no -1.

surfaces.issue: mem_addr must lie in the SFC system bus window 0x200000..0x9FFFFF (vendor SFC_MCPU_START..SFC_MCPU_END), and the flash op must fit inside the mapped chip size. The driver accepts any u32 for both and applies no bounds check, so an out-of-window mem_addr or an over-end flash_addr is silently programmed; on silicon the DMA targets an invalid/aliased address (bus error or silent corruption) instead of being rejected. The vendor returns ERRCODE_SFC_ADDRESS_OVERSTEP / ERRCODE_INVALID_PARAM for these.
surfaces.evidence: fbb_ws63/src/drivers/chips/ws63/porting/sfc/porting/sfc_porting.c:15-16 'SFC_MCPU_START 0x200000 / SFC_MCPU_END 0x9FFFFF'. check_init_param/check_opt_param in fbb_ws63/src/drivers/chips/ws63/porting/sfc/driver/sfc.c:39-58 reject out-of-range mapping/op addresses. PAC bus_dma_mem_saddr doc says valid range 0x00100000~0x00BFFFFF (ws63-pac/src/lib.rs:17451). Driver sfc.rs:400-407 writes both addresses raw, no check.

surfaces.issue: Before every DMA the vendor sets BUS_DMA_AHB_CTRL = 0x7 (INCR4/INCR8/INCR16 burst enables). The Rust driver never writes bus_dma_ahb_ctrl, leaving it at the reset default (bursts off). The DMA is then started with single-beat transfers (or whatever stale value was left), so the transfer is far slower and may not match the AHB master expectation -- an unenforced precondition for a correctly-running DMA.
surfaces.evidence: fbb_ws63/src/drivers/drivers/hal/sfc/hal_sfc_v150_regs_op.h:121-126 'ahb_ctrl.d32 = 0x7;' called from hal_sfc_v150.c:163/186 before set_bus_dma_ctrl. PAC bus_dma_ahb_ctrl incr4/8/16_en bits 0/1/2 (ws63-pac/src/lib.rs:17574-17597). Driver sfc.rs:400-418 never touches it.

surfaces.issue: Vendor DMA path forces dma_sel_cs = 1 (CS1) in BUS_DMA_CTRL; the driver leaves it 0 (CS0). On a board whose flash is wired to the CS the SFC actually drives for bus access this is a silent wrong-chip-select selection for DMA. Lower severity because single-flash boards typically use CS0, but it is an unenforced, board-dependent precondition that the driver hardcodes to a different value than the vendor.
surfaces.evidence: fbb_ws63/src/drivers/drivers/hal/sfc/hal_sfc_v150_regs_op.h:136 'dma_ctrl.b.dma_sel_cs = 0x1;'. PAC dma_sel_cs = bit4 of bus_dma_ctrl (ws63-pac/src/lib.rs:17390-17422). Driver sfc.rs:409-413 sets only dma_start[0] and dma_rw[1], leaving dma_sel_cs=0.

surfaces.issue: cmd_addr is a 30-bit field (bits [0:29]); the driver writes the full u32 via w.bits(address). The top 2 bits of any address >= 0x4000_0000 are silently truncated, so a 4-byte-mode flash address above 1GB lands at a wrapped offset with no error. Reserved/unrunnable for callers passing a full 32-bit flash address. (For typical <=16MB flash this never bites, hence medium.)
surfaces.evidence: PAC cmd_addr is 'FieldWriter<30, u32>', doc 'Bits 0:29 - Operation address' (ws63-pac/src/lib.rs:17827-17839). Driver sfc.rs:298 and :351 write 'w.bits(address)' with the raw u32, no mask. Bits 30-31 are dropped by the field on write.

surfaces.issue: tcss and tcsh are masked with & 0x07 before packing. A caller passing tcss/tcsh >= 8 has the value silently wrapped modulo-8 (e.g. 8 -> 0, 9 -> 1) instead of being rejected. The result is a shorter-than-intended CS setup/hold time programmed with no signal, which can violate the flash's tCSS/tCSH and cause intermittent command failures on silicon.
surfaces.evidence: PAC tcss/tcsh are 'FieldWriter<3>' i.e. 3-bit (ws63-pac/src/lib.rs:16904-16909), 'Bits 8:10' / 'Bits 12:14'; effective time = field+1 (regs_def hal_sfc_v150_regs_def.h:76-80). Driver sfc.rs:155-156 '(tcss as u32) & 0x07' / '(tcsh as u32) & 0x07' -- silent wrap, no Option/Result.

surfaces.field: tshsl (4-bit field, silent mask + floor clamp)
surfaces.issue: tshsl is masked to 4 bits then floored to MIN_TSHSL-2 (=3). This is the previously-flagged case and the mask-then-floor order is now correct (16/32/48 no longer survive). But it is still a SILENT clamp/wrap, not a signalled error: an input of 1 or 2 is silently raised to 3, and an input of 20 is silently masked to 4 -- the caller never learns its requested inter-op delay was not honoured. A construct-time-validated newtype (e.g. Tshsl(u8) with from_field()->Option in 3..=15) would make an unrunnable value unrepresentable instead of silently rewritten.
surfaces.evidence: PAC tshsl 'FieldWriter<4>' (ws63-pac/src/lib.rs:16900-16901), regs_def hal_sfc_v150_regs_def.h:73-74 'Time interval = tshsl + 2'. Driver sfc.rs:154 '((tshsl as u32) & 0x0F).max(MIN_TSHSL - 2)' with MIN_TSHSL=5 (sfc.rs:105). Mask-then-floor is correct but still silent.

surfaces.field: mem_if_type hardcoded Standard for all commands
surfaces.issue: Both send_command and command_with_data hardcode mem_if_type = Standard ('0 << 17') and set no dummy_byte_cnt. A caller cannot issue a dual/quad command via the command path, and more importantly a quad-read instruction passed here is silently executed as a standard-SPI single-line transfer with zero dummy cycles -- returning garbage rather than erroring. This is a representable-but-unrunnable combination (e.g. instruction=0xEB read) that compiles and 'succeeds' (command_done) but yields wrong data. Not a field overflow; a config-surface gap.
surfaces.evidence: PAC cmd_config mem_if_type bits [17:19], dummy_byte_cnt bits [4:6] (ws63-pac/src/lib.rs:17657-17676). regs_def hal_sfc_v150_regs_def.h:242-255. Driver sfc.rs:308 and :363 always '0 << 17' (Standard) and never set dummy_byte_cnt; vendor selects iftype/dummy per command (flash_config_info.c read/write cmd tables).

surfaces.issue: flash_addr_mode[bit2] has a hardware precondition: a write to it has NO EFFECT while CMD_CONFIG[start]=1 (a command is in flight). configure_global writes GLOBAL_CONFIG unconditionally with no check that no command/DMA is in progress, so switching 3-byte<->4-byte addressing during/just-after an op is silently ignored, leaving the controller in the old addressing mode while the caller believes it changed. Unenforced precondition.
surfaces.evidence: PAC doc on flash_addr_mode: 'NOTE: write has no effect when CMD_CONFIG[start]=1' (ws63-pac/src/lib.rs:16774, 16842). Driver configure_global sfc.rs:121-141 writes GLOBAL_CONFIG with no start/busy precondition check.

surfaces.field: XIP-running precondition (reconfigure while executing from flash)
surfaces.issue: The SFC is the boot/XIP flash controller; on a real WS63 image code can be executing in place from the memory-mapped flash window. Toggling AES low-power/IV-valid, asserting/releasing the bus soft-reset, or reconfiguring bus/timing while the CPU is fetching from flash will stall or corrupt the instruction stream (hang/fault). None of these methods enforce or document the precondition that the caller must be running from RAM (not XIP) with the bus quiesced. Class C unenforced precondition rather than a field overflow.
surfaces.evidence: SFC is the XIP/boot flash controller (sfc_porting.c bus window 0x200000..0x9FFFFF is the executable flash mapping). Vendor serializes all ops behind hal_sfc_regs_wait_ready + g_dma_busy guard and runs reconfiguration from RAM (hal_sfc_v150.c:48-69 wait_ready, :148-189 dma busy guard). Driver methods sfc.rs:187-198 (soft reset), 471-489 (AES) write registers with no XIP-safety guard.

audits.notes:
Clock gating: the SFC is the boot/XIP flash controller and is clocked by reset default from the mask ROM (it is what the CPU boots from), so there is NO clock-gate precondition defect here -- clock.rs:78 correctly returns cken_info=None for Peripheral::Sfc and the vendor sfc_porting.c has no clock-enable sequence. I did NOT fabricate a class-C clock-gate finding for SFC (unlike PWM/I2S which self-enable).

Chip gating: all evidence is ws63-specific (ws63-pac + fbb_ws63 porting/sfc/driver). The hal_sfc_v150 core is shared, but the porting (address window 0x200000..0x9FFFFF, read_cmds iftype table) is ws63. BS2X likely differs in the bus window and possibly the iftype table -- not audited here.

Needs on-board confirmation (the type-encodes-measured-reality lesson from PWM):
- The SpiIfType {0,1,2,5,6} encoding (finding 1) is derived from the vendor read_cmds table, which is strong, but whether reserved encodings 3/4 hang vs silently-standard on silicon should be confirmed. The fix is a newtype/enum whose only constructible values are the 5 valid encodings.
- cmd_addr 30-bit truncation (finding, class A) is real per PAC width but only bites for flash >1GB; benign on the 4MB GD25Q32 the SDK targets.
- The bus_dma_len off-by-one (class B) and missing rd/wr_enable (class B) are the highest-confidence, highest-impact silicon bugs: both make the documented operation produce wrong/no result and are directly contradicted by the vendor regs_op. These two warrant the PWM-style construct-time-validated newtypes (a DmaLen(u32) that stores length-1 and rejects 0/over-window, and a BusConfig that cannot be built without the enable bits).

Reference-pattern recommendation: mirror PWM/I2S -- give configure_bus a fallible builder returning Option/Result that (a) only accepts the 5 valid iftype encodings, (b) always sets rd/wr_enable, (c) does RMW preserving BUS_CONFIG2; give bus_dma_start a DmaLen newtype (non-zero, stores n=len-1) and validate mem/flash addr against the 0x200000..0x9FFFFF window; give configure_timing Tshsl/Tcss/Tcsh newtypes with from_*()->Option so out-of-range is unrepresentable rather than masked/floored.

surfaces.issue: configure_channel takes transfer_size as a u16 (0..65535) but masks it with `& 0xFFF` (dma.rs:272) into the 12-bit trans_size field. Any value > 4095 is SILENTLY TRUNCATED to its low 12 bits: e.g. transfer_size=4096 programs 0 beats (no-op transfer that completes instantly copying nothing), 5000 programs 904, etc. No error, no clamp-to-max, no signal. The caller believes a large block was transferred; on silicon the DMA copies a wrapped, wrong length. The vendor explicitly rejects this as an ERROR (transfer_num >= 4096 -> ERRCODE_DMA_RET_TOO_MANY_DATA_TO_TRANSFER), never truncates.
surfaces.evidence: PAC DmacTransSize0W = FieldWriter<'a, REG, 12, u16> (ws63-pac/src/lib.rs:15745); vendor regs_def `transfersize : 12` (hal_dmac_v151_regs_def.h:190); vendor guard `if (base_cfg->transfer_num >= HAL_DMA_CH_MAX_TRANSFER_NUM)` where HAL_DMA_CH_MAX_TRANSFER_NUM=4096 returns error (hal_dmac_v151.c:19, 299-301); driver mask `control |= (transfer_size as u32) & 0xFFF` (dma.rs:272). True valid range: 1..=4095 beats.

surfaces.issue: WS63 has a 4 KB, 32-byte-line, NON-COHERENT D-cache enabled at boot (cache.rs:3). A DMA master sees physical RAM directly. configure_channel writes src/dst addresses and kicks the transfer (dmac_en_chns, dma.rs:322-325) but NEVER performs cache maintenance.
