With structurally-valid CPU-written source data still sitting in dirty cache lines, the DMA reads stale RAM (TX), and after a peripheral/DMA write the CPU reads stale cached values (RX) — the transfer 'completes' but moves wrong/old bytes. The HAL already provides cache::clean_range/invalidate_range/flush_range but the driver does not call them and the doc does not require the caller to. The vendor enable path flushes the whole D-cache before every kick.
surfaces.evidence: Non-coherent D-cache documented in hisi-riscv-hal/src/cache.rs:3-5; vendor `hal_dma_v151_enable` does `osal_dcache_flush_all()` under CONFIG_SUPPORT_DATA_CACHE immediately before setting ch_en (hal_dmac_v151.c:165-170); driver configure_channel/enable_controller perform no clean/invalidate (dma.rs:239-326). cache.rs:60-96 are the available-but-uncalled maintenance ops.

surfaces.issue: DmaChannelConfig exposes src_peripheral and dst_peripheral as public raw u8 (0..255). configure_channel masks each with `& 0x0F` into the 4-bit src_per/dest_per field (dma.rs:297-298). Any value >= 16 is silently truncated to its low nibble: e.g. src_peripheral=16 -> handshaking source 0 (TIE0, no handshake), 17 -> UART_L_TX, etc. The peripheral-paced transfer then handshakes with the WRONG (or no) peripheral request line and stalls or corrupts. Note the vendor MDMA handshaking enum itself runs to index 28 (SPI_M/OPI/QSPI1-3/SPI3/SPI4), and `hal_dma_cfg_set_src_per` writes the raw source straight into the 4-bit field — so those higher IDs are genuinely unreachable; the safe DmaPeripheral enum path only covers 0..14, but the raw u8 fields let a caller pass anything and have it wrap.
surfaces.evidence: regs_def `src_per : 4`, `dest_per : 4` (hal_dmac_v151_regs_def.h:169-170); driver mask `((config.src_peripheral as u32) & 0x0F) << 1` / `& 0x0F) << 5` (dma.rs:297-298); vendor writes raw source into field via hal_dma_cfg_set_src_per(ch, periph_cfg->hs_source) (hal_dmac_v151.c:253-254); MDMA handshaking enum 0..28 in dma_porting.h:56-84 (indices >=16 exceed the 4-bit field). Public u8 fields: dma.rs:133-136.

surfaces.issue: Dma0::CLK_AUTO_CTRL is hardcoded to the WS63 glue register (0x4400_0244, bit 19) with NO #[cfg(feature = "chip-ws63")] gate (dma.rs:63). On WS63 this correctly bypasses the M_DMA auto clock-gate so a started transfer advances. On BS2X (chip-bs2x build, also a target per the driver's chip-both scope), this address is WS63-specific; the BS2X DMA auto-gate / glue register is at a different offset (fbb_bs2x not checked out to cite). enable_controller would then either no-op or poke an unrelated BS2X register, leaving the controller's clock idle-gated -> a configured transfer never completes (done bit never sets), exactly the failure mode the bypass exists to prevent. The whole DmaPeripheral handshaking table is correctly #[cfg(feature=chip-ws63)]-gated, but this clock constant is not.
surfaces.evidence: Dma0 CLK_AUTO_CTRL = Some((0x4400_0244, 0x0008_0000)) with no chip cfg (dma.rs:60-63); matches WS63-only vendor dma_porting.c `#define DMA_CLK_AUTO_CTRL_REG 0x44000244` / `DMA_CLK_ON_MASK 0x80000` (dma_porting.c:28-36). No BS2X equivalent address present in-tree to confirm offset — needs on-board confirmation. DMA has no separate CKEN gate (clock.rs:16 lists Dma/Sdma as cken_info None), so this auto-gate bypass is the controller's only clock provisioning.

surfaces.issue: channel selection is validated by physical_channel_index, which assert!()-PANICS for a channel outside [base, base+4) (dma.rs:79). This DOES signal (good — not a silent truncation), but it panics at runtime rather than being a construct-time-typed channel newtype like the PWM reference pattern. A caller passing channel 4 (MDMA) or 7/12 (SDMA) gets a panic on a no_std target instead of a None/Result. Borderline: it is signalled, not silently unrunnable, so it is the weakest item here — included only because the surface is a writable u8 that can structurally hold an unrunnable value.
surfaces.evidence: physical_channel_index assert `channel >= base && channel < base + 4` (dma.rs:78-81); matches vendor B_DMA_CHANNEL_MAX_NUM=4 / ch%4 convention (dma_porting.h:30, hal_dmac_v151.c:65). Panics rather than returns; no silent wrap.

audits.notes:
Scope: read-only audit of /home/sanchuanhehe/Documents/hispark-rs/hisi-riscv-rs/crates/hisi-riscv-hal/src/dma.rs. No hardware touched.

FIELDS VERIFIED SAFE (no defect — do not flag):
- TransferWidth enum (Width8/16/32 = 0/1/2 only): the swsize/dwsize field is 3 bits but only 0/1/2 are legal widths on WS63 v151; the Rust enum cannot hold an illegal width. PAC DmacSWidth0 enumerates exactly 0/1/2 (lib.rs:15888-15898). The vendor hal_dma.h enum lists WIDTH_64/128/256 (hal_dma.h:53-58) but those are NOT supported by this controller's 3-bit field for width — the Rust type correctly excludes them. SAFE.
- BurstSize enum (Beats1..Beats256 = 0..7): maps 1:1 onto the full 3-bit sbsize/dbsize field; every discriminant 0..7 is a valid burst per PAC DmacSBsize0 (lib.rs:15749-15765). SAFE on MDMA.
- src_addr / dst_addr: full 32-bit fields (PAC DmacSAddr0W/DmacDAddr0W = FieldWriter<...,32,u32>, lib.rs:15704/15608). No truncation. SAFE.
- dmac_config write 0x01: matches vendor hal_dma_cfg_set_en (master1/master2 left 0) (hal_dmac_v151.c:75-81). SAFE.
- control-register bit layout, channel-config bit layout (ch_enable/src_per/dest_per/fc_tt/masks/lock/active@15/halt@16): all match regs_def.h:166-204 exactly. SAFE.
- s_master/d_master hardcoded 0 (M1): matches WS63 dma_port_get_master_select which always returns HAL_DMA_MASTER_SELECT_0 (dma_porting.c:113-118). SAFE on WS63 (may differ if SDMA were ever provisioned).

UNCERTAIN / NEEDS ON-BOARD CONFIRMATION:
1. The BS2X CLK_AUTO_CTRL register offset (item 4) — fbb_bs2x is not checked out locally; could not cite the BS2X auto-gate address. Confirm on a BS2X board whether enable_controller actually ungates the M_DMA clock there, or whether 0x4400_0244 is wrong/harmless on BS2X.
2. SDMA (Sdma0, logical 8-11) is per the codebase memory 'never provisioned on WS63' (CLK_AUTO_CTRL=None). IF it is ever enabled, note the vendor imposes PER-CHANNEL burst-length limits on SDMA channels (g_sdma_burst_len_limit_ch = ch0:4, ch1:4, ch2:8, ch3:32; hal_dmac_v151.c:45-49) — a BurstSize larger than a given SDMA channel's cap is unrunnable on that channel and is NOT enforced by the Rust driver. Deferred/low because SDMA is unclocked today.

RECOMMENDED REMEDIATION (matches the PWM/I2S construct-time-validated-newtype reference pattern):
- transfer_size: introduce a TransferCount(u16) newtype with from_count(u16)->Option capped at 4095 (or a try_from on configure_channel returning Result), so 'a value of the type exists => it is programmable'.
- peripheral selects: drop the public raw u8 fields in favor of the existing DmaPeripheral enum on both sides (the mem_to_peripheral/peripheral_to_mem builders already do this for one side); a raw u8 should not be publicly writable.
- cache: either have configure_channel call cache::clean_range(src)/invalidate_range(dst) itself (self-sufficient like PWM self-enabling its clock), or take cache-maintained buffer wrappers so 'configure => coherent' is guaranteed by construction.
- CLK_AUTO_CTRL: gate the constant per chip feature so BS2X gets its own address (or None) rather than poking a WS63 offset.

audits.driver: misc-sweep (usb.rs, pke.rs, spacc.rs, km.rs, efuse.rs, trng.rs, system.rs, delay.rs)

surfaces.field: implicit clock base = soc::chip::SYSTEM_CLOCK_HZ (240 MHz WS63 / 64 MHz BS21)
surfaces.issue: Delay::new() is a const ZST with NO clock argument; the busy-loop count is derived from the hardcoded SYSTEM_CLOCK_HZ which assumes the PLL is locked. clock_init/soc/ws63.rs explicitly warns 'If the PLL is NOT locked the CPU runs from the TCXO at 24 or 40 MHz. All timing calculations will be wrong.' A caller that constructs Delay before/without PLL lock (or on a 40 MHz-crystal board, or during early boot/XIP where flashboot's clock is inherited) gets a delay that is silently 6x-10x too long (240/24) or too short — no error is signalled. There is no way to pass the actual measured clock. This same Delay is reused inside efuse::write_byte for the AVDD settle, so a wrong clock corrupts an IRREVERSIBLE eFuse program pulse.
surfaces.evidence: soc/ws63.rs:30-33 ('If the PLL is NOT locked ... All timing calculations will be wrong'); SYSTEM_CLOCK_HZ=240e6 (soc/ws63.rs:33) / 64e6 (soc/bs21.rs:25); delay.rs:22 `SYSTEM_CLOCK_HZ as u64 * us / 1_000_000`. Vendor uses a HW timebase (uapi_tcxo_delay_us, hal_efuse_v151.c:119/127) not a CPU busy-loop. PWM precedent: drivers must encode the actual clock, not the datasheet CPU clock.

surfaces.issue: The loop count assumes exactly 3 CPU cycles per iteration, but the body is core::hint::spin_loop() which on RISC-V lowers to a pause/nop hint with no guaranteed cycle cost, and the empty loop is subject to compiler scheduling. The 3-cycle assumption is not silicon-calibrated, so even with a correct clock the realized delay can be off by a large constant factor. Not signalled. (Lower severity than the clock-base issue but compounds it.)
surfaces.evidence: delay.rs:20-23 (`cycles_per_loop: u32 = 3`); body is only `core::hint::spin_loop()` (delay.rs:25). No measured-on-silicon calibration constant exists (contrast PWM, which encodes measured hardware reality).

surfaces.issue: The field is full-width 8-bit so there is no truncation, BUT the value is a calibration constant that MUST match the live TCXO frequency: vendor uses 0x29 @ 24 MHz and 0x19 @ 40 MHz. set_clock_period accepts ANY u8 (including 0, the reset value) and silently programs it. A value that does not match the board crystal yields out-of-spec eFuse access timing — reads return garbage / programs under-burn. Worse, the method is OPTIONAL: read_byte/write_byte do not call it and do not verify it was set, so a caller can read/program eFuse with the reset value 0 (no working access timing). This is the unenforced-precondition the vendor satisfies in pm_porting/efuse_porting before any access.
surfaces.evidence: efuse_clk_period field width 8 (ws63-pac lib.rs:24720, bits[0:7]); vendor required values EFUSE_24M_CLK=0x29 / EFUSE_40M_CLK=0x19 chosen by get_tcxo_freq() (efuse_porting.c:115-124, pm_porting.c:272-279); set_efuse_clock_period() runs in pm init before any access (pm_porting.c:639). The HAL doc itself says 'call before any read/program' but nothing enforces it.

surfaces.issue: The address is computed as `start_byte + i as u16` and only the per-byte `byte_addr >= EFUSE_MAX_BYTES(256)` check in read_byte guards it. For a large start_byte the u16 sum WRAPS (release) or PANICS (debug): e.g. read_buffer(65530, &mut [_;12]) — addresses 65530..65535 correctly return OutOfRange, but the wrap then yields 0,1,2,3,4,5 which silently pass the < 256 check and read the WRONG region of the array. The vendor checks the whole span up front: `(byte_number + length) > EFUSE_MAX_BYTES` is missing here.
surfaces.evidence: efuse.rs:121 `start_byte + i as u16`; reproduced wrap: start=65530,len=12 -> 0,1,2,3,4,5 accepted as in-range. Vendor up-front span check efuse.c:74-75 `(byte_number + length) > EFUSE_MAX_BYTES`. EFUSE_MAX_BYTES=256 confirmed (efuse_porting.h:25-28: 2048 bits/8).

surfaces.field: AVDD program-voltage settle timing via crate::delay::Delay busy-loop
surfaces.issue: The irreversible program pulse raises AVDD, waits EFUSE_PROGRAM_DELAY_US(100) via the CPU busy-loop Delay (see Delay finding), then lowers AVDD. The vendor uses a hardware TCXO delay (uapi_tcxo_delay_us). If the CPU clock differs from the assumed SYSTEM_CLOCK_HZ (PLL not locked / wrong crystal), the AVDD-on window is too short or too long, under-burning or stressing a ONE-TIME-PROGRAMMABLE fuse with no way to detect failure. Additionally there is no readback/verify after the burn and no clock-precondition check.
surfaces.evidence: efuse.rs:137,142,147 use crate::delay::Delay (CPU busy-loop) for the AVDD settle; vendor uses uapi_tcxo_delay_us(HAL_EFUSE_DELAY_US=100) around AVDD set/clear (hal_efuse_v151.c:35,118-127). Program is irreversible (driver doc efuse.rs:21-22).

surfaces.issue: Field is full-width 8-bit (no truncation), but the value range that yields working entropy is constrained: the vendor hard-codes 0x1b (27) as the FRO output-clock divider and the default is 0x1b. A small divider (e.g. 0 or 1) samples the free-running oscillator far too fast, so the FRO entropy fails the quality gate and read_blocking() spins to permanent Timeout — a structurally-valid value that produces no working output. set_divider lets the caller program any u8 with no validation against the usable range.
surfaces.evidence: fro_div_cnt width 8, default 0x1b (ws63-pac lib.rs:29245, 'default 0x1b'); vendor fixes TRNG_INNER_FRO_DIV_CNT=0x1b and writes it during init (hal_trng_reg.h:25, hal_trng.c:58-60); divider semantics ('Divide the frequency of the output clock of the FRO ring' hal_trng.c:57). Low/zero dividers are outside the validated point.

surfaces.issue: set_sample_clock(true) selects the EXTERNAL sample clock. The vendor init ALWAYS forces the INTERNAL FRO clock (TRNG_INNER_FRO_CLK) and never selects external. If a caller selects external but no external sample clock is provisioned/routed on the board, the FRO is never sampled, no entropy is produced, and every read_blocking() returns Timeout. This is an unenforced board-provisioning precondition exposed as a plain bool.
surfaces.evidence: fro_sample_clk_sel '0=inner; 1=external' (ws63-pac lib.rs:29201, bit 0); vendor forces inner: clk_sel.bits.fro_sample_clk_sel = TRNG_INNER_FRO_CLK and only writes when it isn't already inner (hal_trng.c:53-56). No external clock is wired in the vendor path.

surfaces.issue: The read path returns the raw FIFO word the instant data_ready is set, WITHOUT the vendor's mandatory entropy sanity checks. The vendor rejects 0x00000000, 0xFFFFFFFF, and two consecutive equal reads as failed/stuck entropy and keeps polling. The HAL surfaces these degenerate 'random' values to the caller as success — on a cold/unstable FRO this silently yields non-random (all-zero / all-one / repeated) bytes. Not a numeric-field overflow but a config/read surface that is 'structurally valid, silently unrunnable as a CSPRNG source'.
surfaces.evidence: trng.rs:57-58 / 70-71 return fifo_data with no value check; vendor rejects `*randnum == 0x00000000 || == 0xffffffff || == chk_randnum` and re-reads, only succeeding otherwise (hal_trng.c:111-113).

surfaces.field: hardcoded WS63 absolute MMIO: CHIP_RESET_REG=0x4000_2110, SYS_RST_RECORD_0=0x4000_00A0, SYS_DIAG_CLR_1=0x4000_00A4
surfaces.issue: These are WS63-specific GLB_CTL_M / GLB_CTL absolute addresses with NO chip gating, yet the audit/crate targets BOTH WS63 and BS2X. On BS2X the GLB_CTL block base/offsets differ, so on a BS2X build software_reset() sets bit 2 of an address that is not the BS2X chip-reset register — it may fail to reset (hang in the spin loop) or write a foreign MMIO with side effects, and reset_reason() reads/clears the wrong record. Compiles cleanly for both chips; unrunnable (or harmful) on BS2X.
surfaces.evidence: system.rs:42-45 hardcodes 0x4000_2110 / 0x4000_00A0 / 0x4000_00A4 with the source comment 'from fbb_ws63 .../ws63/porting/reboot/reboot_porting.c' (WS63 only, system.rs:38). No `#[cfg(chip-...)]` exists in system.rs. Vendor reboot path is per-chip porting (drivers/chips/ws63/porting/reboot/reboot_porting.c); no bs2x equivalent was found at the same addresses.

audits.notes:
SAFE / no real issue (do not flag): (1) usb.rs has NO caller-writable config surface — Usb::new() takes only the peripheral token; devspd=0 (high speed) and devaddr=0 are hardcoded, both valid 2-bit/7-bit values; speed_from_enumspd is total over u8. The only real precondition (PHY/PMU power-on + USB clock) is explicitly documented as 'assumed done by the boot/clock layer' (usb.rs:11-12) and is not exposed as a writable field, so it is out of scope for a config-surface defect though worth an on-board check. (2) pke.rs and spacc.rs are STUBS — enable()/disable()/is_busy() are empty/return-false no-ops with no register writes and no config fields; nothing is writable-but-unrunnable because nothing is writable. The doc claims clock enable ('Enable clock via the security clock gate') but the body is empty — a missing-clock-gate (class C) latent issue ONLY once these are implemented; today there is no surface. (3) km.rs is SAFE: slot is u8 guarded by assert!(slot < KEYSLOT_COUNT=8); the key_slot_num/slot_num_cfg fields are 10-bit (ws63-pac lib.rs:28672/28948), so slot<8 can never overflow; lock_cmd/type bits are correct.

NEEDS ON-BOARD CONFIRMATION: (a) the Delay clock-base assumption (PLL locked) and the 3-cycles/loop calibration — must be measured on silicon for both 240 MHz WS63 and 64 MHz BS21, and for the 40 MHz-crystal board variant. (b) Whether efuse access actually fails with clk_period=0 vs merely degraded (recommend measuring). (c) BS2X chip-reset register address to confirm system.rs portability. (d) TRNG minimum usable fro_div_cnt and whether external sample clock is ever wired.

RECOMMENDED PATTERN (per the PWM precedent): introduce construct-time-validated newtypes — e.g. EfuseClkPeriod::for_tcxo(TcxoFreq)->{0x29|0x19} (no free u8), FroDivCnt::from_count(u8)->Option rejecting the unrunnable low range, an EfuseAddr/EfuseSpan newtype that validates start+len <= 256 up front (kills the read_buffer wrap), a Delay that takes the live Clocks (probe_clocks result) instead of the const SYSTEM_CLOCK_HZ, and #[cfg(chip-...)] gating (or a typed reset register) for system.rs. TRNG read should apply the vendor 0/0xffffffff/duplicate quality gate before returning.

surfaces.issue: Driver writes `cols.saturating_sub(1)` straight into the 3-bit clo_pin_en field. PAC bits() does `value & mask::<3>()` (silent truncation, no panic). Vendor regs_def says valid column values are 0-7 (i.e. cols 1..=8, KEYSCAN_MAX_COLUMN=8). A caller passing cols=9 writes 8=0b1000 which masks to 0b000 -> the matrix collapses to 1 column; cols=10 -> 0b001 = 2 columns. The construction compiles and looks valid but silently programs the WRONG geometry on silicon (the whole right portion of the key matrix is never scanned). saturating_sub does NOT bound the upper end.
surfaces.evidence: PAC bs2x-pac/src/lib.rs:24240 `CloPinEnW = FieldWriter<...,3>`; lib.rs:384-388 bits() masks silently; vendor hal_keyscan_v150_regs_def.h:165-166 `clo_pin_en :3 valid 0-7`; driver keyscan.rs:45 `w.clo_pin_en().bits(cols.saturating_sub(1))`; KEYSCAN_MAX_COLUMN=8 in keyscan.c:54

surfaces.issue: Driver writes `rows.saturating_sub(1)` into the 5-bit row_pin_en field. Vendor regs_def says valid row values are 0-17 (rows 1..=18). The field width is 5 bits (0..31) so rows 19..=32 are accepted by the type but exceed the silicon's physical row count (18) -> those phantom rows never produce events; rows>32 silently wrap mod 32 (rows=34 -> 33=0b100001 -> 0b00001 = 2 rows). No clamp at 18 and no None at construction -> structurally valid but unrunnable geometry.
surfaces.evidence: PAC lib.rs:24244 `RowPinEnW = FieldWriter<...,5>`; vendor hal_keyscan_v150_regs_def.h:168-169 `row_pin_en :5 valid 0-17`; driver keyscan.rs:44

surfaces.field: row/col GPIO pin mux (HAL_PIO_KEY_SCAN) + pull config + KEYSCAN_PIN_SEL routing
surfaces.issue: The driver enables the scanner but never muxes any GPIO to the keyscan function, never sets row pull-down / col pull-up, and never writes the KEYSCAN_PIN_SEL_ROW/COL routing registers that map logical rows/cols to physical pins. The vendor does ALL of this in keyscan_porting_pin_set (called from hal init before enable). Without it the scanner samples whatever the pins default to (GPIO/floating) -> no key ever decodes; read_key only ever returns the FIFO-empty marker. Unenforced board/pinmux precondition: `new` claims 'configures + starts' but the output path is dead.
surfaces.evidence: vendor hal_keyscan_v150.c:45 calls keyscan_porting_pin_set(); keyscan_porting.c:173-205 sets uapi_pin_set_mode(HAL_PIO_KEY_SCAN), PIN_PULL_DOWN/UP, reg16_setbits(KEYSCAN_PIN_SEL_ROW0_REG/COL_REG); Rust keyscan.rs:38-53 has no pinctrl at all

surfaces.field: scan timing: pulse_time / scan_mode / wait_time / idle_time / defence / io_de
surfaces.issue: Driver writes ONLY keyscan_pin_num then enable+clk+start; it never programs pulse_time, scan_mode, wait_time, idle_time, defence_num or io_de. The vendor programs all of these (non-trivial: pulse=10us, wait=30+5us, idle=3ms, 3-sample defence, io_de=true) before enable. Left at IP reset (likely 0), the row strobe pulse / inter-scan wait can be zero so the matrix is never driven long enough to register a press, and debounce is off -> at best unreliable, at worst no events. Construction compiles and starts but produces no working scan on silicon.
surfaces.evidence: vendor hal_keyscan_v150.c:46-53 config_pulse_time/mode/direction/wait_time/idle_time/defence/io_de from KEYSCAN_DEFAULT_CONFIG (keyscan_porting.h:155-165: EVERY_ROW_PULSE_10_US, WAITING_30_AND_5_US, IDLE_3_MS_AFTER_SCAN, SAMPLE_3TIMES_DEFENCE, io_de=true); Rust keyscan.rs sets none of these registers

surfaces.field: PDM clock-source mux + CRG global gate (pdm_port_clock_enable)
surfaces.issue: new() writes ONLY the PDM-local clk_rst_en register at 0x5208E000. It never selects the PDM functional clock source (clear bit1 of 0x5200004c to pick the XO crystal instead of the RC osc) and never opens the CRG global gate (set bit10 glb_clken_pdm of 0x52000548). The vendor does both in pdm_port_clock_enable(true) BEFORE set_attr. Without the global gate the whole PDM block is unclocked; with the wrong source mux it runs off the imprecise RC clock. Result: capture() spins on up_fifo_empty until POLL_LIMIT and returns 0 samples. Unenforced clock precondition - construction does NOT yield a clocked block.
surfaces.evidence: vendor pdm_porting.c:23-32 pdm_port_clock_enable: uapi_reg_clrbit(0x5200004c,0x1)=XO sel, uapi_reg_setbit(0x52000548,10)=glb_clken_pdm; hal_pdm_v150.c:22 calls it; Rust pdm.rs:34-61 touches neither 0x5200004c nor 0x52000548

surfaces.field: per-stage datapath clocks: cic_clken_0 / srcdn_clken_0 / hpf_clken_0 / compd_clken_0 / ckdiv_6144k_clken / saradc_clken / clk_freq_sel
surfaces.issue: new() enables only dmic_clken, up_fifo_clken, func_up_en, func_up_ch_en_0 (4 of ~13 clk_rst_en bits). The vendor set_attr additionally enables cic_clken_0, srcdn_clken_0, hpf_clken_0, compd_clken_0 (the CIC/SRC/HPF/compensation filter-stage clocks), ckdiv_6144k_clken (the 6.144 MHz master-clock divider that generates the DMIC bit clock), saradc_clken, and sets clk_freq_sel=0. With the filter-stage clocks gated off there is no decimation and with ckdiv_6144k off the mic gets no clock -> the UP FIFO never fills. Structurally-valid construction, no working audio output.
surfaces.evidence: vendor hal_pdm_v150.c:47-73 sets srcdn_clken_0/hpf_clken_0/compd_clken_0/cic_clken_0/ckdiv_6144k_clken/saradc_clken/clk_freq_sel; all these fields exist+writable in PAC clk_rst_en (bs2x-pac lib.rs cic_clken_0, srcdn_clken_0, ckdiv_6144k_clken, saradc_clken, clk_freq_sel); Rust pdm.rs:51-58 writes only 4 bits

surfaces.field: AFE / DMIC pad provisioning + cic_gain / srcdn_mode hardcoded (no config surface)
surfaces.issue: new() takes NO caller parameters - decimation gain (0x14), srcdn_mode (0=32->16kHz), FIFO thresholds are all hardcoded constants, so there is no field a caller can over-range here (good). The real gap is an unenforced board precondition: the DMIC data/clock pads must be muxed to PDM and (for analog AMIC) the AFE/mic-bias provisioned; the driver assumes this. cic_gain is 8-bit (PAC FieldWriter<8>) so the constant 0x14 is in range; srcdn_mode is 2-bit and 0 is valid. No overflow, but the construction is silently non-functional without external AFE/pad setup.
surfaces.evidence: PAC lib.rs:25088 CicGain0W=FieldWriter<8> (0x14 fits), srcdn_mode_0=FieldWriter<2>; vendor pdm_amic peripheral sample provisions AFE separately; Rust pdm.rs:39-49 hardcodes all config, no pad/AFE setup

surfaces.field: QDEC clock select + enable (M_CTL_QDEC_CLK_CTL 0x520004A0) + sample period
surfaces.issue: new() writes only qdec_enable + qdec_task_start at the QDEC block (0x5200_0200). It never runs qdec_clk_sel_set: select the clock source (1M or 32k) and SET the clock-sel-enable bit (QDEC_CLK_EN_BIT) in M_CTL_QDEC_CLK_CTL_REG at 0x520004A0, program the freq divider, and write the sample period (M_CTL_QDEC_SAMPLEPER_REG). Without an enabled clock the decoder never samples the A/B inputs -> qdec_acc_data stays 0, so read_count() always returns 0. Unenforced clock precondition; construction does not yield a sampling decoder.
surfaces.evidence: vendor qdec_porting.c:90-108 qdec_clk_sel_set: reg16_setbit(M_CTL_QDEC_CLK_CTL_REG, QDEC_CLK_EN_BIT), writew sampleper, hal_qdec_freq_div; QDEC_CLK_DIV_ADDR=0x520004A0 (qdec_porting.c:21); clk-div field qdec_clk_div num:7/en:1 (hal_qdec_v150_regs_def.h:393-398); Rust qdec.rs:24-30 writes neither M_CTL nor sampleper

surfaces.field: QDEC A/B GPIO pin mux (HAL_PIO_QDEC_A/B) + input dir + pull-up
surfaces.issue: new() never muxes the encoder A/B pins to the QDEC function, never sets them to input, drive strength, or pull-up. The vendor qdec_port_pinmux_init does all of this and the sample calls it BEFORE uapi_qdec_init/enable. Without the pin mux the decoder reads whatever the pads default to (GPIO/floating) -> no valid gray-code transitions, ACC never moves. Structurally-valid construction with a dead input path.
surfaces.evidence: vendor qdec_porting.c:74-88 qdec_port_pinmux_init: uapi_pin_set_mode(HAL_PIO_QDEC_A/B), set_dir INPUT, set_pull UP; qdec sample calls it before enable (samples/.../qdec line ~31); Rust qdec.rs has no pinctrl

surfaces.issue: new() skips the entire vendor hal_qdec_v150_init config block: set_sampleper (the sampling period - controls how often A/B are latched), debounce filter (defen_en/defen_num), report period, LED config. With sampleper left at reset (likely 0) the decoder may never complete a sample, and with debounce off mechanical bounce double-counts. The construction starts the task but is not configured to decode reliably.
surfaces.evidence: vendor hal_qdec_v150.c:22-59 init sets sampleper, defen_en/defen_num, reportper, ledpol before clr_en; Rust qdec.rs:24-30 sets none of these

audits.notes: Cross-cutting reality check needed on hardware. (1) None of the three drivers expose a numeric Config struct with caller-tunable rates/dividers — the only caller-supplied numerics are keyscan rows/cols, which is exactly where the silent register-field truncation lives (defect A, the only true overflow surface). PDM/QDEC take no config args (gains/modes/thresholds hardcoded), so their defects are all clock/precondition (C) and under-configuration (B), not field overflow. (2) The dominant theme vs the PWM reference pattern ('construct -> clocked'): all three FAIL the self-enable-clock contract. PWM self-enables CLK_SEL+CKEN+DIV; here Keyscan omits pin mux + scan timing, PDM omits the CRG glb gate (0x52000548 bit10) + clock-src mux (0x5200004c bit1) + 7 datapath sub-clocks, QDEC omits the M_CTL clock-enable (0x520004A0) + pinmux + sampleper. A faithful port should fold pdm_port_clock_enable / qdec_clk_sel_set / keyscan pinmux into new(). (3) Pin-mux defects (C) are arguably out of these drivers' scope if a separate GPIO/pinctrl layer is expected to mux pads first — confirm whether the HAL has a pinctrl path that callers must invoke; if not, these are real silent-dead-output bugs. (4) Recommendation mirroring PWM/I2S: replace Keyscan::new(rows,cols) with validated newtypes (e.g. RowCount(u8) from_count -> Option bounded 1..=18, ColCount 1..=8) so the 3-bit/5-bit truncation becomes a None at construction instead of a silent wrap. (5) Every clock-gate claim (CRG bit positions, M_CTL offsets) is from the vendor porting C and needs on-board confirmation that the bits latch (cf. the PWM pwm_freq_h 'does not latch on silicon' lesson) before trusting the datasheet widths — flagged for HIL verification.
